accessibility:
The accessibility command provides comprehensive configuration options for players using screen readers, assistive technology, or who need visual accommodations. Typing 'accessibility' alone opens a settings form where you can configure all options at once. The main accessibility mode optimizes game output for screen readers and enables automatic text-to-speech queuing. Screen reader optimization simplifies formatting and removes unnecessary visual elements that confuse assistive technology.

Additional features include high contrast mode for visual readability, reduced visual effects to minimize animations, and detailed TTS controls. You can configure whether narration pauses when you start typing and auto-resumes when you stop, adjust playback speed from 0.5x to 2.0x, and control exactly when the system reads output aloud. Individual settings can be toggled via commands like 'accessibility mode on', 'accessibility contrast on', or 'accessibility speed 1.5'. Use 'accessibility status' to see all current settings and 'accessibility help' for a complete command reference. These features ensure the game is fully playable regardless of accessibility needs.
activate location:
When you create new locations in the game world, they start in an inactive state to prevent premature access. This command lets you activate those locations, making them available for players to visit and interact with. If you have multiple inactive locations, the system presents a menu where you can choose which one to activate.

This is particularly useful after building new areas or neighborhoods—once you've finished setting up rooms, exits, and decorations, use this command to officially open the location to the game world. Only your own recently-created locations appear in the activation list.
activity:
The activity system powers collaborative group challenges, missions, and adventures. This comprehensive command manages every aspect of participating in structured group activities—from browsing available missions to making strategic choices during rounds.

Activities unfold in rounds where participants choose actions, spend willpower for bonuses, help each other, or recover resources. Different round types offer different options: standard rounds let you choose from predetermined actions, branch rounds let the group vote on which direction to take, rest rounds let you heal and decide whether to continue, free roll rounds use AI to evaluate creative actions, and persuade rounds let you attempt to convince NPCs through roleplay.

The command includes subcommands for listing activities, starting/joining/leaving them, checking status, choosing actions, helping other players, spending willpower dice, and interacting with round-specific mechanics. It's a full cooperative adventure system that combines tactical choice-making with collaborative storytelling.
afk:
The afk command marks you as 'away from keyboard' to let other players know you're temporarily unavailable for roleplay or interaction. When you go AFK, your character visually 'gets busy with their phone' and others see this status when they look at you or check the who list. You can optionally specify how many minutes you'll be gone with 'afk [minutes]' for automatic return, or just use 'afk' for indefinite away status.

Using the command again toggles it off - you 'put away your phone' and are marked as active again. This is essential multiplayer etiquette, letting others know not to expect immediate responses from you and helping coordinate roleplay scenes. Players respect AFK status and won't typically try to engage AFK characters in active scenes. The system tracks your AFK state and duration, and if you specified a time limit, it will automatically clear after that period elapses.
aobserve:
While activity participants fight battles or explore dungeons, you can observe from outside and influence the outcome. This command lets you request to either support or oppose a participant in their activity, creating dramatic moments where allies and rivals can affect the action remotely. The participant must accept your request before you become an active observer.

Once accepted, you can queue powerful actions that trigger during activity rounds—stat swaps, advantage bonuses, disadvantage penalties, or custom roleplay effects depending on the round type. Your role (supporter or opposer) determines which actions are available to you. This system creates interesting dynamics where players can help friends succeed in dangerous missions or create complications for rivals, all while maintaining player agency through the consent-based request system.
arrangescene:
Staff members use this command to arrange private meeting scenes between NPCs and player characters. You specify which NPC will meet which PC, where the initial meeting happens, and optionally where the actual roleplay scene takes place. The system creates an arranged scene record, sends an invitation to the PC, and sets up the framework for the interaction.

This is particularly useful for plot-driven encounters, quest initiations, or important story moments where staff want to connect players with specific NPCs at specific locations. After creating the scene, staff can add instructions for the NPC using the sceneinstructions command, guiding how the NPC should behave and what information they should share.
attack:
The attack command initiates and manages combat encounters. When you're not in a fight, using 'attack [target]' will challenge that character or creature to combat, immediately beginning a tactical battle with turn-based rounds. Once combat begins, you'll be presented with a menu of combat options for each round.

If you're already in a fight, the attack command lets you change your target mid-combat by specifying a different opponent, or confirm your attack action for the current round. The combat system uses a sophisticated quickmenu interface that walks you through choosing your main action (attack, defend, use ability), movement options, and tactical positioning. Combat happens in discrete rounds where everyone locked in their choices, then all actions resolve simultaneously with detailed narrative descriptions of strikes, blocks, damage taken, and tactical consequences. The command ensures you're alive and standing before allowing combat, and respects timeline rules where death may be disabled in certain historical periods.
attempt:
Request permission before performing an action on another character, respecting player boundaries and enabling consent-based roleplay. When you use this command, the target character receives a menu where they can allow or deny your proposed action. This creates a respectful environment where physical interactions require mutual agreement.

Use this for any action that involves touching or directly affecting another character—hugs, kisses, handshakes, or anything else that requires their participation. The system queues only one attempt at a time per character, preventing spam and ensuring each request gets proper consideration. Once the target responds, the interaction can proceed naturally through regular emotes or your action is respectfully declined.
autogm:
The autogm command starts AI-driven spontaneous adventures that react dynamically to your actions. Type 'autogm start' to begin an adventure based on your current location and nearby world memories - the AI Game Master analyzes context and creates a custom adventure scenario. You can include party members with 'autogm start with <names>'. The system designs multi-stage adventures with inciting incidents, complications, and resolutions. The AI observes your commands (say, emote, look, etc.) and responds with narrative developments, NPC interactions, and challenges.

Adventures track chaos levels that rise with bold actions, feature combat encounters, and have 2-hour activity timeouts. Check progress with 'autogm status' to see current stage, countdown timers, and recent events. End adventures with 'autogm end' (abandoned), 'autogm end success' (victory), or 'autogm end failure' (defeat) - only the initiator can end an adventure. Successful adventures create world memories that influence future adventures in that area. This system provides solo or group PvE content without staff involvement, perfect for when you want structured gameplay but no pre-scheduled events are available.
balance:
The balance command displays a complete overview of your character's finances, showing both the cash you're carrying in your wallet and any money stored in bank accounts. The output is organized into sections: first your wallet contents (cash on hand), then your bank account balances, and finally a total sum across all sources. If you use multiple currencies in the game world, each currency is listed separately with properly formatted amounts.

This is essential for tracking your wealth before making purchases, planning major expenses, or simply keeping tabs on your finances. Your wallet balance represents physical money you're carrying and could potentially lose if robbed, while bank balances are safely stored and accessed through banking commands. You can also use shorter aliases like 'bal', 'money', 'cash', or 'wallet' to quickly check your funds during shopping or negotiating business deals.
bb:
The bulletin board serves as a public message center where characters can post announcements, look for groups, advertise services, or share news with the community. Think of it as a central noticeboard where anyone can read recent posts and contribute their own messages.

Each character can maintain one bulletin at a time—posting a new one automatically replaces your previous post, keeping the board from getting too cluttered. You can list all recent bulletins, read specific ones by number, post your own messages, or delete your current bulletin. It's perfect for finding adventure companions, advertising your shop, announcing events, or just staying connected with what's happening in the game world.
blindfold:
Place a blindfold or hood over a helpless character's eyes, completely blocking their vision. This restraint command requires the target to already be in a helpless state (bound, unconscious, or otherwise unable to resist). Once blindfolded, the character cannot see room descriptions, other characters, or visual events happening around them.

Blindfolding creates interesting roleplay scenarios where the victim must rely on other senses and communication to understand what's happening. The darkness adds tension and vulnerability to prisoner scenarios, interrogations, or surprise situations. Combined with other restraints, it forms part of the comprehensive captivity mechanics.
bounce:
As an event host or staff member, remove a disruptive attendee from your event immediately. When you bounce someone, they're permanently excluded from that specific event and cannot return even if they leave and come back to the location. This provides hosts with the tools they need to maintain the quality and safety of their gatherings.

The command broadcasts the removal to everyone present and notifies the bounced character of their exclusion. Use this when someone is causing problems, violating event rules, or making other attendees uncomfortable. It's a targeted moderation tool that affects only that one event, not the character's access to other areas or activities.
broadcast:
Send urgent announcements to every player in the game simultaneously. This staff-only command delivers your message to all currently online players instantly, and offline players receive it as a notification when they next log in. Use it for critical server notices like upcoming maintenance, major game events, policy changes, or emergency announcements.

The broadcast system ensures no player misses important information—your message persists for offline players and appears as a high-priority system notification. Keep broadcasts concise and use them sparingly to maintain their impact when something truly important needs community-wide attention.
build:
The unified entry point for all building and construction commands in the game. Instead of remembering dozens of different building command names, just type 'build' to see a menu of everything you can create or modify. The menu adapts to your location and permissions, showing only relevant options—for example, you'll only see the option to build a city if you're in a location without one, or to build a block if you're at an intersection.

Options are organized into two categories: Create (new cities, blocks, rooms, shops, apartments) and Modify (edit, rename, resize, decorate, or delete existing structures you own). Each option routes to the appropriate specialized command, making the build system discoverable and easy to navigate even for newcomers to world-building.
build apartment:
Find an available apartment in the city, or if none exist with your preferred specifications, the system automatically creates a new apartment tower and assigns you a unit. You can optionally specify a size preference (small, medium, large, or penthouse) and the command will try to match your request.

When an apartment is found or created, you're presented with details about the unit and given the choice to claim it as your home, just visit it without claiming, or cancel. Claiming an apartment sets it as your home room, allowing you to use the 'home' command to return there from anywhere in the game. This makes getting started with housing effortless—no need to manually search or coordinate with staff.
build block:
Transform a city intersection into a developed block with buildings and interior rooms. This powerful building command supports dozens of building types (from apartment towers and brownstones to churches and hospitals) and multiple layout options (single building, split layouts, quadrants, perimeter developments, and mixed-use configurations).

You must be standing at an intersection in a city to use this command. Specify the building type and optionally a layout style—for example, 'build block brownstone' creates a single brownstone filling the block, while 'build block cafe quadrants' creates four corner cafes. The system generates not just the building exteriors but fully navigable interior spaces appropriate to each building type, complete with proper dimensions and floor layouts.
build city:
Create a complete city infrastructure with a street grid, intersections, and building sites ready for development. This staff-only command transforms a location into an urban environment by generating a network of east-west streets and north-south avenues, with intersections at every crossing where buildings can be constructed. You can specify the grid size (number of streets and avenues), maximum building heights, and optionally use AI to generate thematic street names.

Once built, the city becomes a navigable space where players can build blocks at intersections using the 'build block' command. The system automatically creates the street rooms, links them with proper spatial navigation, and marks intersections as buildable spaces. Cities are permanent investments in world infrastructure—you can't build another city on top of an existing one, so plan carefully before committing.
build location:
Create a new location (standalone building) with its own entry room, instantly becoming the owner of this new space. The command automatically moves you into the entry room of your new location, ready to customize and expand it. This is the starting point for creating personal spaces like homes, shops, headquarters, or any other standalone building that exists as its own distinct location.

Unlike rooms created within existing buildings, locations created this way exist independently in the zone's hierarchy. You can expand them by adding more rooms using other building commands, decorate them, set up shops, or configure them however you like. The location belongs to you, giving you full control over access and modifications within its boundaries.
build shop:
Transform your current room into a retail space by establishing a shop there. This command creates the shop infrastructure that lets you stock items and sell them to other players. You must own the room to create a shop in it, ensuring you have control over your commercial space. Once established, use inventory management commands to add stock, set prices, and manage your business.

The room automatically becomes marked as a shop type, and a shopkeeper record is created to handle transactions. Give your shop a name to establish its brand, or leave it unnamed for a generic marketplace feel. This is your first step in becoming a merchant—after creation, you'll use additional commands to populate your inventory and conduct business.
buy:
Purchase items from shops you encounter throughout the game world. If you just type 'buy' without specifying an item, you'll see a convenient menu showing everything the shop has for sale, complete with prices and stock levels. You can buy single items or specify a quantity (like 'buy 3 potions'), and if multiple items match your search, the system presents a disambiguation menu.

Payment is processed automatically from your wallet and bank account, with the system checking your bank first (unless it's a cash-only shop) before drawing from your pocket money. The shop's inventory updates immediately, preventing overselling, and everyone in the room sees when you make a purchase. This makes shopping feel natural and integrated into the game world rather than being a separate interface.
buy house:
Houses are major purchases that require careful consideration and configuration through the web interface. Rather than buying them through quick in-game commands, you browse available properties on the real estate page where you can see detailed layouts, locations, pricing, and customization options before committing to such a significant investment.

This command exists to inform you about the proper purchase process and direct you to the web interface where you can explore your housing options thoroughly. Think of it as a helpful pointer toward the rich property management system available through your browser.
buy shop:
Purchasing a shop is a significant business decision that requires the detailed configuration tools available in the web interface. The commercial real estate page lets you browse available shop locations, examine their foot traffic, review lease terms, and configure your storefront before making this major economic commitment.

Like house purchases, shop transactions need the full web interface to give you access to inventory management setup, pricing configuration, and other business tools. This command simply directs you to where these sophisticated economic features live.
buy vehicle:
Vehicles come with extensive customization options—colors, features, performance upgrades—that need the comprehensive tools available through the web dealership interface. Rather than limiting you to quick command-line purchases, the web page lets you configure exactly the vehicle you want before spending your money.

This command serves as a helpful redirect, pointing you toward the proper vehicle purchase workflow where you can explore all available models, compare specifications, and design your dream ride with the full configurator.
camera:
As an event host or staff member, direct audience attention by putting a 'spotlight' on specific characters. When spotlighted, a character's actions become highlighted or emphasized, making it clear to everyone present that this person is currently the focus of the event. You can toggle the spotlight on and off freely, or set it to automatically turn off after a specific number of emotes.

This is perfect for performances, presentations, ceremonies, or any structured event where you want to manage the flow of attention. The spotlight is a soft social signal—it doesn't restrict anyone's ability to act, but it helps participants understand where to direct their focus during organized activities.
cancelscene:
Cancel arranged scenes that are still in the pending state (before they've been accepted or started). This staff command cleans up scene invitations that are no longer needed, such as when plans change or an NPC meeting is no longer relevant. You must specify the scene ID number, which staff can find through the scene management interface.

Note that you can only cancel pending scenes—once a scene has been accepted and is active, you must use 'endscene' instead. This distinction protects active roleplay from accidental interruption while allowing staff to maintain clean scene queues.
cards:
The cards command opens an interactive menu for playing card games. When you type 'cards', a quickmenu appears with options for various card game actions like getting a deck, dealing cards, drawing from the deck, playing cards, or discarding. This provides an organized interface for card game mechanics rather than requiring you to remember specific command names for each action.

Card games are useful for roleplay scenes in taverns, casinos, or social gatherings where characters would naturally play cards. The menu-driven interface makes it accessible even if you're not familiar with all the individual card commands. Once you select an action from the menu, the game guides you through the specifics. This is perfect for poker games, blackjack, or any card-based gambling or entertainment scenarios where you want mechanical support for the roleplay.
carry:
Pick up and carry a helpless character, moving them with you as you travel through the game world. This command requires the target to be in a helpless state (bound, unconscious, or otherwise unable to resist). Once you're carrying someone, they move automatically wherever you go, staying in your custody until you set them down.

This is particularly useful for rescue scenarios, moving injured allies to safety, or maintaining control over captives during prisoner roleplay. The carried character is effectively under your control for movement purposes, making this a powerful but consent-requiring action—you can't carry someone who isn't helpless.
change name:
Change your character's nickname, forename (first name), or surname (last name). Your nickname can be changed anytime with no restrictions, but forename and surname changes have a 21-day cooldown period to prevent abuse. The command automatically checks for name uniqueness - you can't choose a forename/surname combination that's already taken by another character.

Names are automatically capitalized for consistency. Nicknames have no cooldown and can be changed freely, making them perfect for informal names or titles your character goes by. Examples: 'change name nickname Bobby', 'change name forename Robert', 'change name surname Smith'.
channel:
The channel command lets you participate in global or group chat channels that connect players across the entire game world. Unlike room-based communication, channels let you chat with everyone subscribed to that channel regardless of physical location. Each channel has a specific purpose - there might be an OOC channel for general chatter, a help channel for questions, channels for specific player groups or factions, or channels for different game worlds.

You use it with 'channel [name] [message]' like 'channel ooc Hello everyone!' to broadcast to all members of that channel. There's also a convenient shortcut: '+' followed by your message sends to the default OOC channel without typing the channel name. Public channels automatically add you as a member when you first speak, while private channels require you to join first. The system tracks your last used channel for status display, and you can see available channels with the 'channels' command. If you're muted in a channel, you won't be able to send messages until the mute is lifted.
channels:
Browse all communication channels available in the game, seeing at a glance which ones you've joined, which you've muted, and how many people are currently online in each. Channels are organized by type (public, clan, staff, etc.) and displayed with their names, descriptions, and your current membership status.

This command is your gateway to the game's communication infrastructure—after viewing the list, you can join interesting channels with 'join channel', send messages with the channel command, or use the quick 'ooc' command for out-of-character chat. It's perfect for discovering active communities and finding the conversations you want to be part of.
check in:
Control your visibility in the 'where' command, determining who can find your location when they search for online players. You have three privacy options: visible to everyone (default), visible only to players who've marked you as a favorite, or completely hidden from the where list.

This privacy control respects player boundaries while maintaining some administrative oversight—staff with special permissions can still locate you if needed, but regular players only see you according to your preference. Adjust this setting based on whether you want to be easily found for roleplay, prefer privacy from strangers, or only want to be visible to friends.
checkall:
Activate emergency abuse monitoring across all players, bypassing the normal exemption for experienced players. Normally, the abuse detection system only monitors new players (under 100 hours playtime), trusting that veterans have demonstrated their good behavior. This command removes that exemption temporarily, subjecting everyone to the same scrutiny.

Use this during suspected waves of coordinated abuse, bot attacks, or when established players might be involved in rule violations. You can specify a duration (1-24 hours, default 1 hour) and a reason for the activation. All staff are immediately notified when this override is activated, creating a logged audit trail. The enhanced monitoring automatically expires after the specified duration, or can be cancelled early with 'checkalloff'.
checkalloff:
Cancel the abuse monitoring override activated by 'checkall', immediately returning to normal monitoring rules where experienced players (100+ hours) are exempt from automated abuse detection. This staff command notifies all staff members when the override is cancelled, maintaining the audit trail and providing transparency about moderation actions.

Use this when the situation that required enhanced monitoring has been resolved, or when you determine that the full-scope monitoring is no longer necessary. The system identifies who originally activated the override, creating accountability even when different staff members activate and deactivate it.
clan:
The clan command is your central hub for managing guild and group memberships. Typing 'clan' alone shows help for all available subcommands. You can view available clans ('clan list'), create your own ('clan create <name>'), and add '--secret' to make hidden clans that only members know exist. Leaders and officers can invite players ('clan invite <name>'), optionally assigning them a handle/alias with 'clan invite <name> as <handle>'. You can kick members, leave clans you're in, and view detailed info with 'clan info' or member rosters with 'clan roster'.

Secret clans use handles instead of real names for anonymity - set yours with 'clan handle <alias>'. Clans can have their own private communication channels accessible via the 'channel' command. Clan officers can grant and revoke room access, allowing clan members to enter private properties. To communicate with your clan, send mass messages using 'clan memo <subject>=<body>' which all members receive. If you're in multiple clans, many commands prompt you to choose which clan you're referring to. This system enables organized group roleplay, from criminal syndicates to noble houses to professional guilds.
clean graffiti:
The clean graffiti command removes all graffiti from a room you own, scrubbing the walls clean. Simply type 'clean graffiti' when standing in your property that has graffiti on it. The system checks room ownership before allowing the cleaning, ensuring only property owners can remove graffiti from their walls. All graffiti in the room is removed at once - there's no way to selectively remove specific pieces while keeping others.

Other players in the room see you scrubbing the graffiti from the walls when you use this command. The action takes effect immediately and permanently - once cleaned, the graffiti is gone and cannot be recovered. This is useful when your property has been vandalized, when you want to maintain a clean aesthetic, or when old graffiti no longer fits your desired atmosphere. If there's no graffiti present, the command tells you so rather than doing nothing. This gives property owners control over the appearance and messages displayed in their spaces.
close roof:
Close the convertible roof of the vehicle you're currently in, sealing the cabin from the elements. This command only works when you're inside a convertible vehicle with an open roof—regular vehicles or those with hardtops won't respond to this command since they don't have operable roofs.

Closing the roof affects the vehicle's description and may provide protection from weather effects or environmental hazards. All occupants of the vehicle see when the roof is opened or closed, making it a shared environmental control rather than a private action.
combat:
The combat command is an accessibility-focused information hub that provides text-based combat status and tactical recommendations during fights. When you type 'combat' alone or 'combat status', you see a comprehensive overview of the current battle including your HP, enemies' status, allies' status, turn order, and any ongoing effects. The command has several subcommands: 'combat enemies' lists all hostile combatants with their HP and distance, 'combat allies' shows friendly fighters, and 'combat recommend' uses AI to suggest the best target based on threat level, HP, and positioning.

Additional subcommands include 'combat actions' which lists what you can do this turn, 'combat menu' for a quickmenu interface, and 'combat help' for command reference. All output is optimized for screen readers with clear formatting, explicit distances in hexes, and HP shown as current/max ratios. This command is essential for visually impaired players or anyone who wants a clear tactical overview without having to remember various combat commands. It doesn't perform actions itself - it just provides information to help you make informed decisions about what attack, defend, or move commands to use.
commands:
The commands command is your master list of everything you can do in the game, organized by category for easy discovery. When used without arguments, it shows you all available command categories in a two-column table, making it easy to browse different areas of functionality at a glance. Categories include things like combat, navigation, communication, inventory, social, entertainment, and more.

You can drill down into specific categories with 'commands [category name]' to see a detailed list of every command in that category along with brief descriptions of what each does. For example, 'commands navigation' shows all movement and positioning commands, while 'commands combat' lists fighting-related abilities. The command marks unavailable commands (those you can't currently use due to requirements or context) with an open circle, while available commands get a filled circle. This is your starting point for discovering the game's capabilities - use this to explore, then use 'help [command]' to learn the details of any specific command that interests you.
cover:
Discreetly hide visible items you're wearing, concealing them from others' view. This is perfect for concealing weapons, badges, identification, or anything else you don't want noticed. Unlike most commands, covering items doesn't broadcast to the room—it's meant to be a subtle action that happens without drawing attention.

You can cover single items or multiple items at once by separating them with commas. Once concealed, items remain hidden until you explicitly reveal them again. This makes it ideal for maintaining a low profile, hiding your affiliations or equipment, or preparing for situations where you want certain possessions kept private.
create event:
Organize an event—party, meeting, performance, tournament, or any other scheduled gathering. You can either open a detailed creation form that lets you configure every aspect (time, location, type, privacy, attendee visibility), or quick-create with just a name, which defaults to starting in one hour at your current location with public settings.

Events serve as the organizational backbone for scheduled activities in the game. They provide RSVP management, attendee lists, automated notifications, and special host powers like spotlighting or bouncing disruptive attendees. Once created, you'll be automatically added as the first attendee and can use additional event commands to manage it.
decorate:
Add decorative descriptions to rooms you own, bringing them to life with personal touches and atmosphere. Each decoration you add appears as a separate line in the room description, allowing you to build up rich, layered environments. You might add "A faded tapestry hangs on the north wall" or "Candles flicker on every available surface, casting dancing shadows."

Decorations are displayed in the order you add them, so you can craft a natural flow from general atmosphere to specific details. Unlike the room's base description, decorations can be easily added to or changed over time, making them perfect for seasonal changes, story events, or simply refining your space as your vision evolves.
delete:
The delete command removes various types of content you've created, with the specific item type determined by what you're trying to delete. Currently it supports two main uses: 'delete bulletin' removes your character's bulletin board posts (all of them at once), and 'delete place <name>' removes furniture or points of interest you've created in rooms you own. The command is intentionally conservative to prevent accidental deletions - you must own the room to delete places in it, and there's no undo function.

When deleting places, if you don't specify which place, the command shows you a list of all places in the room with numbers, helping you identify what's there before deletion. This is useful for removing old furniture when redecorating or clearing out clutter from your property. The command may expand in the future to support deleting other types of content. Always double-check before deleting, as these actions are permanent. Use 'delete bulletin' to wipe your bulletin board clean when you want to start fresh with new posts.
delete location:
Clear your saved home location, removing the association between your character and any specific room. Once cleared, the 'home' command will no longer work until you set a new home location. This is useful when moving permanently to a new area, when your previous home is no longer available, or when you simply want to start fresh with navigation.

Currently this command only supports clearing your home location, with planned future support for managing other saved location bookmarks. The command tells you what your previous home was before clearing it, providing confirmation of what you're removing.
delete room:
Permanently remove a room you own from the game world, along with all its decorations, descriptions, and associated data. This is a destructive action with safety checks: you can't delete a room if other players are present, if it's the only room in its location, or if you don't own it. Before deletion, the system moves you to the parent room, reparents any nested rooms, and relocates any items that were inside.

Use this for removing failed experiments, cleaning up temporary spaces, or demolishing rooms you no longer need. The command ensures data integrity by finding a safe destination for everything that was in the deleted room, preventing orphaned content or broken references.
delete zone:
This administrator-only command completely removes a zone and everything it contains from the game—all locations, rooms, shops, decorations, characters' possessions, the works. Before deletion, the system automatically relocates every character (online and offline) who was in that zone to a spawn room, notifying online players of the move. The deletion is thorough and irreversible, removing tens of thousands of database records in one operation.

Use this only when retiring entire game areas, cleaning up test zones, or performing major world restructuring. The command provides a summary of what was deleted (location count, room count, characters relocated) and creates an audit trail of the administrative action. Never use this command while standing in the zone you're deleting—move to safety first.
delve:
Enter procedurally generated dungeons with time-limited challenges, fog of war exploration, and tactical decision-making. Each delve is a self-contained adventure with a 60-minute time limit where you navigate through grid-based levels fighting monsters, collecting treasure, solving puzzles, and dealing with traps and obstacles. Your HP, willpower, and time are precious resources you must manage carefully.

The system features sophisticated mechanics: timed sequence traps you must observe and time correctly, skill-check obstacles you can make easier with effort, combat encounters where you can study monsters for bonuses, puzzles that reward clever thinking, and a minimap that reveals terrain as you explore. Actions consume time (10 seconds to move, 5 minutes to fight, 30 seconds to focus), and the procedural generation ensures every delve is unique. When time runs out or you flee, you keep any loot you've collected and earn rewards based on your performance.
deposit:
The deposit command transfers physical cash from your wallet into your bank account for safe storage. You must be standing at a bank location or ATM to deposit - you can't do it remotely. Type 'deposit' followed by an amount like 'deposit 100', or use 'deposit all' to put all your cash into the bank. The system enforces a minimum deposit amount, and obviously you can't deposit more than you're carrying.

Money in your bank account is completely safe from theft or loss, unlike cash in your wallet which you could lose if robbed or killed. Other characters will see you deposit cash but won't see the exact amount. After depositing, the game shows your new bank balance. Use 'balance' to check both your wallet and bank totals, and 'withdraw' when you need physical cash again. Banking is essential for protecting your wealth while still having spending money available.
describe:
The describe command opens your character's description editor, a graphical interface where you can craft and manage your character's physical appearance. This is where you write the text that others see when they look at you - your height, build, facial features, style, and distinguishing characteristics. The editor allows you to create multiple description paragraphs that paint a vivid picture of your character.

Rather than editing descriptions through clunky command-line text entry, the describe command launches a proper editing interface (typically a web panel or popup window) where you can compose, preview, and refine your character's appearance description with full text editing capabilities. This is part of the broader customization system that lets you define how your character appears to others in the game world. Changes you make in the editor are immediately reflected when other players use the 'look' command to examine you.
design:
A comprehensive staff tool for creating any type of item in the game world—weapons, armor, clothing, furniture, containers, food, and more. The design command opens a form-based interface where you can specify all the properties of the item you're creating, from basic details like name and description to specialized attributes like damage dice for weapons or capacity for containers.

The system automatically adapts the form based on the type of item you're creating, showing only relevant fields. For example, weapons get fields for damage dice and weapon type, while furniture gets seating capacity and position options. This unified interface replaces the need for separate item creation commands, making it easy for staff to populate the world with diverse, properly-configured objects.
diceroll:
Roll custom dice using standard RPG notation—specify the number of dice, their sides, and optional modifiers. The command broadcasts animated dice rolls to everyone in the room, complete with visual effects showing each die's result and the final total. Support for the full range of standard dice notation: simple rolls like '2d6', single die checks like '1d20', or modified rolls like '3d8+5'.

The dice animation system creates engaging visual feedback, showing each individual die result before calculating the total. Results are logged in the room's message history, providing a permanent record of all rolls. This makes it perfect for resolving chance-based decisions, settling disputes, running informal games, or just rolling dice for fun.
directory:
The directory command displays a business directory for your current zone, showing all shops and commercial establishments in the area. When you type 'directory', you see a categorized list of businesses grouped by location (villages, towns, neighborhoods), with each entry showing the shop name and the specific room where it's located. This is like a yellow pages or business directory that helps you find where to shop for goods and services.

The directory only shows businesses within your current zone (a large geographic area), making it practical rather than overwhelming. Once you find a shop you're interested in, you can navigate to that location and use the 'list' command to see what they sell. Future versions may support category filters like 'directory food' or 'directory clothing' to narrow results. This command is essential for new players learning what's available in their area and for anyone looking for specific types of shops without having to wander around exploring.
discord:
The discord command configures webhook-based notifications to send game updates directly to your Discord server or channel. When set up, you'll receive Discord messages when important things happen in-game while you're offline (or online if you enable that too). Typing 'discord' alone opens a settings form. You can configure which types of events trigger notifications: memos sent to you, private messages, roleplay mentions, and more.

To set up notifications, you need a Discord webhook URL from your server's settings (Server Settings > Integrations > Webhooks). Use 'discord webhook <url>' to configure it, then 'discord test' to verify it works. You can enable separate toggles for offline notifications, online notifications, memos, PMs, and mentions - giving you fine control over what disturbs you. Use 'discord status' to see your current configuration. The system respects your online status and privacy preferences, never sending information about other players' actions unless they directly involve you. This keeps you connected to the game even when you can't actively play.
down:
Move in the specified cardinal direction to an adjacent room. This is one of the basic movement commands that lets you navigate through the game world. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat - use fight commands instead.

Each direction command (north, south, east, west, up, down, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest) works the same way. Just type the direction name or its abbreviation (n, s, e, w, u, d, ne, nw, se, sw). Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names.
drag:
Drag a helpless character along the ground, moving them forcibly as you travel. Like carrying, this requires the target to be in a helpless state (bound, unconscious, or otherwise unable to resist), but dragging suggests a less gentle approach than carrying—you're hauling them along rather than supporting them. The character moves automatically wherever you go, staying under your control until you release them.

Dragging includes timeline verification, making it useful for historical roleplay scenarios where you need to transport helpless characters through different time periods. It's mechanically similar to carrying but with different flavor—choose dragging for rougher handling or situations where carrying doesn't fit the narrative tone.
dress:
The dress command lets you put clothing from your inventory onto another character, like dressing a friend in a jacket or placing a hat on someone's head. The syntax is 'dress <character> with <item>' - for example, 'dress Alice with jacket'. This requires the other player's consent through the game's permission system. If they haven't pre-approved dress actions from you, they'll receive a quickmenu asking whether to allow it.

The item must be clothing or jewelry in your inventory, and the target character can't already be wearing that specific item. If permission is granted, the item transfers to them and is automatically worn. Everyone in the room sees you dressing them. This command is great for costume changes during scenes, helping someone get dressed, or collaborative styling. It respects privacy boundaries - players can block dress interactions from specific people in their privacy settings. To dress yourself, just use the regular 'wear' command instead.
drink:
The drink command lets you consume beverages from your inventory, creating immersive drinking moments where your character takes time to actually drink. When you use this command, your character begins drinking and others see the action, with support for roleplay-friendly aliases like 'sip', 'gulp', and 'quaff' that add flavor to how you consume liquids.

The drinking process happens progressively over time rather than instantly, allowing for realistic scenes where characters share drinks, toast together, or are interrupted mid-sip. Beverages in the game can have various properties - refreshing water, energizing coffee, intoxicating alcohol, or special potions with magical effects. The system tracks your drinking state and broadcasts appropriate messages to observers, making beverage consumption a social and narrative element rather than just a mechanical action.
drive:
Get in your vehicle and drive to a specified destination, using the same pathfinding and navigation system as walking but at vehicle speed. The command finds your vehicle (either one you're already in or one you own in the current room), resolves where you want to go, and starts an automated journey. If your vehicle isn't in the current room, the system tells you where it's parked so you can retrieve it.

Driving uses the vehicle travel service to handle movement, which accounts for vehicle speed, fuel consumption, and road network when applicable. You can drive to named locations, intersections, or use the same destination syntax as walking. This makes vehicles practical for long-distance travel or moving quickly through familiar areas.
drop:
The drop command lets you remove items from your inventory and place them on the ground in your current room. You can drop specific items by name ('drop sword'), drop money ('drop 100'), or empty your entire inventory at once ('drop all'). If you use the command without specifying what to drop, you'll get a helpful menu showing everything you're carrying.

The command is smart about ambiguity - if you're carrying multiple similar items, it will ask you to clarify which one you mean. When you successfully drop something, everyone else in the room sees an action message describing what you did. This is useful for sharing items with others, lightening your load, or setting up objects in a room for roleplay scenes.
dry off:
Use this command to dry yourself off when you're wet. You'll need a towel in your inventory to use this command. The command automatically finds an available towel and uses it to dry off, updating your wetness state. Other characters in the room will see you drying off.

This is useful after swimming, getting caught in the rain, or any other situation that leaves you wet. The command is simple - just type 'dry off' or 'dry' and it will handle the rest.
east:
Move in the specified cardinal direction to an adjacent room. This is one of the basic movement commands that lets you navigate through the game world. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat - use fight commands instead.

Each direction command (north, south, east, west, up, down, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest) works the same way. Just type the direction name or its abbreviation (n, s, e, w, u, d, ne, nw, se, sw). Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names.
eat:
The eat command lets you consume food items from your inventory, with the eating process happening over time in an immersive way. When you eat something, your character begins eating and others in the room see you consuming the food. The command supports various verb aliases like 'consume', 'taste', and 'swallow' for roleplay variety.

The consumption system is progressive - you don't instantly devour food, but rather your character state reflects that you're in the process of eating. This creates realistic roleplay moments where characters can be interrupted mid-meal, finish eating at a natural pace, or share meals together. Food items in the game can have various properties and effects that trigger when consumed, from simple nourishment to special buffs or flavor text describing the taste and experience of the meal.
edit room:
Opens a pop-out editor window that lets you modify the properties of a room you own. You can edit the room's name, short description (shown in room lists), long description (shown when you look at the room), room type, and background image URL. The editor provides a convenient form interface for making these changes.

You must be the owner of the room to edit it. The command validates that names and descriptions are within reasonable length limits, and checks that background URLs are properly formatted. This is essential for customizing your private spaces and making them feel unique.
emote:
The emote command is your creative tool for expressing actions, emotions, and non-verbal communication that enrich roleplay scenes. Unlike 'say' which is for speaking, emote describes what your character does - smiling, waving, frowning, dancing, or any other physical action you can imagine. The emote appears to everyone in the room with your character's name automatically inserted at the beginning.

You can use several aliases for variety: 'emote waves', 'pose stretches', ':grins', or 'act fidgets nervously'. The command supports adverbs for added description and will intelligently place them for natural reading. Emotes are saved in roleplay logs and can trigger reactions from AI-powered NPCs who respond to your actions. The system includes rate limiting in crowded rooms to prevent spam, and staff members have access to special 'emit' mode that shows messages without a character name attached (useful for narrating environmental events). Speech within emotes can be colored using your custom speech color for added visual flavor, and offline characters mentioned in your emotes may receive Discord notifications.
end event:
Officially end an event that you're hosting. This command is only available to the event organizer and can only be used while you're currently in the event. When you end an event, all attendees are notified, the event status is updated to completed, and everyone is removed from the event. The event remains in the system as a completed event for record-keeping.

You cannot end an event that has already been completed or cancelled. This command provides a clean way to formally conclude an event once activities are finished. Example: 'end event'.
endscene:
Use this command to end an arranged scene and return all participants to the original meeting location. This is the proper way to conclude a scene that was started with the 'arrangescene' command. When you end a scene, everyone involved is automatically transported back to where they were before the scene began.

Only the scene organizer or staff members can end a scene. This ensures that scenes aren't abruptly terminated without the agreement of whoever set them up. After ending, you'll see the room description of your return location.
enter:
Move into a named location or building. This command is useful when you want to enter a specific place by name rather than just moving in a direction. For example, 'enter tavern' or 'enter shop' will take you into that location if it's adjacent to where you're standing.

The command uses intelligent name matching to find the location you're trying to enter. When you enter successfully, the game announces your departure to people in your current room and your arrival to people in the destination room, using personalized names (people see names based on whether they know you or not). This is particularly useful in urban areas with many buildings and named locations.
enter event:
The enter event command joins an active event happening at your current location. Simply type 'enter event' and you'll join whatever event is currently running in the room. If you're the event organizer and the event is scheduled but hasn't started yet, using this command automatically starts it. The system tracks attendance and notifies everyone already in the event that you've arrived.

You can only enter one event at a time, and you can't enter if you've been bounced (removed) from that event by the host. Events create a special shared experience - while inside, your actions and messages are primarily seen by other event participants, creating an intimate scene even in busy locations. Events are perfect for parties, competitions, meetings, or any organized gathering. Use 'leave event' when you're ready to depart, which returns you to the normal room environment.
enter game:
The enter game command transitions you from the tutorial Exit Hall into the main game world. Once you've completed the tutorial and are standing in the Exit Hall, type 'enter game' to step through the shimmering portal. You'll be transported to New Haven village, the starting location where new adventurers begin their journey. The game provides immersive flavor text describing your arrival at the crossroads with sensory details like the smell of fresh bread and sounds of a blacksmith.

This is a one-way journey that marks your graduation from the tutorial into the full game world. Other players in the Exit Hall will see you vanish through the portal, and you'll appear in New Haven ready to explore. You can only use this command from the Exit Hall - if you try it elsewhere, the system reminds you to complete the tutorial first. After entering the game, you'll have access to all locations, can interact with established players, and begin building your character's story in the persistent world.
equipment:
The equipment command shows you a focused view of what you're currently wearing on your body and holding in your hands - your 'equipped' items that are actively visible and ready for use. This is different from your full inventory, which includes everything you're carrying in pockets or bags. Equipment displays items in two organized sections: 'In Hand' for held items and 'Wearing' for clothing, armor, and jewelry.

The display is formatted with HTML for readability, stacking identical items together with quantity counts and showing item condition and damage states in subdued text so you can quickly assess what needs repair. This command is particularly useful before combat (to verify you're holding your weapon), before social encounters (to check your outfit), or when you need to quickly see what equipment you have ready without wading through your full inventory. It's the at-a-glance answer to 'what am I wearing right now?'
eta:
The eta command shows your journey status when you're traveling across the world map between distant locations. Type 'eta' during a long journey to see your estimated time of arrival, destination name, vehicle type (horse, cart, train, airship, etc.), current terrain you're passing through, and how many hexes remain until you reach your destination. This gives you real-time feedback on long-distance travel that takes significant time to complete.

World travel in Firefly uses a hex-based world map system where journeys between far-apart locations aren't instant - you actually spend time traveling, during which you can chat OOC, plan your arrival, or just wait. The eta command lets you check progress without having to guess how much longer the journey will take. If you're not currently on a journey, the command tells you so. This only applies to world-scale journeys between zones or regions, not local movement within a city or zone. Use 'stop journey' if you need to cancel the travel and disembark at your current position.
event info:
View detailed information about a specific event, including its status (scheduled, in progress, or completed), event type, host, location, start and end times, and current attendees. The command shows different information and options depending on your relationship to the event - organizers see management options, staff see administrative tools, and attendees see participation options.

This is your main command for checking on events you're interested in or managing events you've created. It provides a comprehensive overview of everything happening with the event, including who's planning to attend and where it will take place.
events:
The events command displays an interactive calendar of upcoming events in the game world. Type 'events' alone to open a menu showing the next 10 upcoming events with their locations and attendee counts. Active events show a bullet (●) indicator. The menu also provides shortcuts to create new events, view events you're attending ('events my'), or see events at your current location ('events here'). Each event listing includes the host, event type, start time, and current attendance.

You can create your own events directly from the menu or by typing 'events create', which opens a form where you specify the event name, description, type (party, meeting, competition, concert, ceremony), whether it's public or private, and when it starts. Events help coordinate group roleplay scenes and social gatherings. Other players can see upcoming events on the calendar and plan to attend. Use 'enter event' when you arrive at an event location to join the festivities, and 'leave event' when you're done.
exits:
The exits command shows you all available paths out of your current room, displayed with directional arrows and the names of destination rooms. This gives you a quick snapshot of where you can go without having to look at the full room description. The system uses spatial navigation - rooms connect based on their physical layout and open walls, so exits are automatically determined by room geometry.

The display includes cardinal directions (north, south, east, west), diagonal directions (northeast, northwest, etc.), vertical directions (up, down), and special 'enter' options for rooms contained within your current location (like buildings in a street, or closets in a bedroom). Each exit shows a Unicode arrow for visual clarity (↑ for north, → for east, etc.) followed by the direction name and the destination room in parentheses. This command is essential for navigation planning and understanding the layout of your surroundings.
expose:
Reveal one or more items that you've previously hidden or concealed. This command makes concealed items visible again to everyone in the room. Unlike the 'cover' command which operates silently, exposing items broadcasts a message to the room so others can see you revealing something.

You can expose multiple items at once by listing them separated by commas or 'and'. For example, 'expose knife' or 'expose knife and dagger'. This is useful when you want to make hidden items visible again, whether for dramatic effect or practical purposes.
fabricate:
Create items from patterns you own using appropriate crafting facilities. This is the main command for manufacturing clothes, weapons, jewelry, tattoos, pets, and other items in the game. You must have the pattern in your inventory and be at an appropriate facility (tailor for clothes, forge for weapons, etc.). The crafting process may be instant (in sci-fi settings) or take time (in historical settings).

For timed crafting, you can choose between waiting to pick up the item at the workshop or having it delivered to your home when ready. Use 'fabricate' alone to see your pending orders, 'fabricate pickup <number>' to collect a ready order, or 'fabricate <item name>' to start crafting. You can also use 'fabricate deck' to create card decks from patterns you own. The command provides era-appropriate messaging based on whether you're in medieval, modern, or sci-fi settings.
fight:
The fight command initiates full combat with another character or creature, entering a turn-based tactical battle system where participants can be injured or killed. When you engage someone in combat, both fighters are locked into an interactive combat menu where you choose targets, select actions (attack, defend, use abilities), and manage movement across the battle map each round.

If you're already in a fight and use this command again, it will reopen your combat menu so you can review your options and change your choices before the round resolves. The command presents a quickmenu if you don't specify a target, showing everyone in the room available to fight. Combat is serious and consequential - characters can lose HP, be knocked out, or even die depending on the game's timeline settings. Everyone in the room sees the combat initiation and each round's resolution with detailed narrative descriptions of strikes, blocks, movement, and tactical positioning.
finger:
The finger command provides detailed out-of-character information about another character, including online status, last activity, and schedule compatibility. When you finger someone, you see their full name, race, class, level, whether they're currently online, and their last activity timestamp. If they're online, you'll see their exact location and posture. If they're offline, you'll see when they were last seen. This helps you find people for roleplay or check if friends are around.

Uniquely, finger also shows whether you 'know' this character (have been formally introduced in-game) and displays schedule overlap data - the system analyzes when you're both typically online and shows what percentage of time your play schedules align. If there are particularly good days or times when you're both active, it highlights those common windows. This feature helps players coordinate scenes and understand why they might be missing each other. The command searches first in your current room, then globally across the game, so you can finger anyone you've met regardless of where they are.
flash:
The flash command creates a theatrical moment where you briefly reveal a concealed item - like flashing a hidden badge or weapon - and then immediately conceal it again. This is purely a roleplay gesture; the item remains concealed afterward. You might use this to show identification to a guard, give someone a glimpse of a hidden weapon as a threat, or any situation where you want to momentarily reveal something you're keeping hidden.

The command only works on items you're wearing that are already concealed. Everyone in the room sees you flash the item, but since it's just a quick glimpse, they might not get full details. This creates dramatic moments without permanently exposing your concealed equipment. If you want to actually make something visible long-term, use 'expose' instead. Flash is perfect for tense scenes where characters are hinting at what they're carrying without fully revealing their capabilities.
follow:
The follow command makes your character automatically trail behind another character as they move through the game world. When you follow someone, you'll move with them whenever they walk to a new location - there's no need to manually type each movement command. Just type 'follow' followed by the character's name, and you'll start following them. If multiple characters have similar names, the game will present a menu so you can choose which one you meant.

Following requires the other player's permission through their privacy settings - if they've blocked follow requests from you, you'll receive an error message. While following, you can see where the leader goes and participate in scenes together, making it great for group travel, escorts, or social roleplay. To stop following, simply use the 'stop' or 'stop following' command. Following automatically ends if you manually move somewhere else or if you log out.
gag:
Apply a gag to a helpless character to prevent them from speaking. This is a restraint command that only works on characters who are helpless (unconscious, tied up, or otherwise incapacitated). Once gagged, the target character cannot use speech-based commands like say, shout, or whisper until the gag is removed.

This command is part of the prisoner/restraint system and requires your target to already be in a helpless state. The action is announced to everyone in the room, and the target receives a message that they've been gagged. Example: 'gag Bob'.
gen:
Generate content using AI-powered world building. This command provides access to powerful LLM-based generation tools for creating room descriptions, backgrounds, NPCs, items, places, and even entire cities. You must have building permissions to use generation commands. Most generation jobs run asynchronously in the background, giving you a job ID to track progress.

Available generation types: 'gen description' (room description), 'gen seasonal' (seasonal variants), 'gen background' (room image), 'gen npc [role]' (generate NPC), 'gen item [category]' (create item), 'gen place [type]' (generate location like tavern), 'gen city [size]' (generate full city). Use 'gen jobs' to see your active generation jobs, 'gen job <id>' to check status, and 'gen job <id> cancel' to cancel a running job. The system adjusts terminology based on the current era (craftsman's workshop in medieval, fabrication facility in sci-fi).
get:
The get command lets you pick up objects from the ground and add them to your inventory. You can specify exactly what you want ('get sword'), pick up money from the room floor ('get money' or 'get 100'), or gather everything at once ('get all'). If you don't specify what to get, the command will show you a menu of all available items in the room.

The command is smart about item matching - if there are multiple similar items and your request is ambiguous, it will ask you to clarify which one you mean. When you successfully pick something up, everyone else in the room sees an action message describing what you did. The command handles both individual items and bulk operations efficiently, and it automatically filters out money items when showing regular object lists, letting you manage cash separately from physical objects.
give:
The give command lets you transfer items or money from your inventory to another character in the same room. You use it with the format 'give [item] to [person]', such as 'give sword to John' or 'give 100 to Alice'. The command supports many natural verb aliases like 'hand', 'offer', 'throw', 'toss', 'pass', 'slip', and 'gift' for added roleplay flavor.

When you don't specify what to give, the command presents a menu of your possessions and the people in the room, making it easy to browse and select. The transfer is immediate and visible to everyone present - both the recipient and other observers see appropriate action messages describing the exchange. You can give individual items by name or money by amount (just the number), and the command is smart about finding the right target even with partial name matches. Note that you cannot give things to yourself, and the recipient must be present in the same room as you.
give number:
Give your phone number to another character who is in the same room with you. This allows them to contact you via phone commands even when you're not in the same location. The command checks if the target already has your number to prevent duplicates. You cannot give your number to yourself.

When you give someone your number, the action is visible to everyone in the room. The recipient will then be able to use phone-related commands to contact you. Examples: 'give number Bob', 'give number to Alice'.
goto:
The goto command is a staff-only teleportation tool that lets game masters, administrators, and staff members instantly travel to any room by ID number or to any character's current location by name. When you use goto, you vanish from your current location 'in a shimmer of light' and instantly appear at your destination with the same dramatic effect, visible to everyone in both rooms.

Staff can use this for rapid response to player requests, investigating issues, monitoring scenes, attending to multiple areas quickly, or traveling to characters who need assistance. The command accepts either a numeric room ID ('goto 42') to jump to a specific room, or a character name ('goto Bob') to teleport directly to wherever that character currently is, whether they're online or offline. This bypasses all normal navigation restrictions and geography, making it an essential administrative tool for managing the game world.
graffiti:
The graffiti command lets you write messages on walls in rooms where you have modification permissions. Type 'graffiti' followed by your text (like 'graffiti Kilroy was here' or 'graffiti FOR A GOOD TIME CALL...') to scrawl something on the wall at your current position. The system enforces a maximum character length to keep graffiti manageable. Other players in the room see you scrawling something on the wall, but they won't see what you wrote until they look around and notice the graffiti.

Graffiti persists in the room at the specific coordinates where you wrote it, becoming part of the room's description and atmosphere. It's perfect for leaving messages, marking territory, creating urban decay ambiance, or adding character to abandoned spaces. Room owners (or those with appropriate permissions) can remove graffiti with the 'clean graffiti' command. Graffiti is visible to everyone who looks around the room, making it a form of public expression or vandalism depending on context. The feature adds gritty realism to urban settings and lets players leave their mark on the world literally.
grant room:
Grant permanent access to a room you own to another character. This command lets you give someone the ability to enter a locked room within your property, even when you're not there. You must be in the specific room you want to grant access to, and you must own that room. The access is permanent until manually revoked.

The command checks if the character already has access before creating a new grant. This is useful for giving trusted friends or tenants access to specific rooms in your buildings. Note that this grants access to the specific room, not necessarily your entire property. Examples: 'grant room Bob', 'grant room access Alice'.
gtg:
Set or clear your "got to go" (GTG) status, which signals to other players that you'll be leaving soon. Specify the number of minutes until you need to leave (default is 15 minutes if not specified, capped at 1000 minutes). When you set GTG status, other characters in your room see a message that you received a notification on your phone indicating you'll be leaving.

Using 'gtg' without arguments clears your GTG status if it's already set. This is a courtesy feature to let other players know they should wrap up any ongoing roleplay or interactions. The status is visible to others and provides a countdown. Examples: 'gtg' (set 15 minutes), 'gtg 30' (set 30 minutes), 'gtg' (clear if already set).
help:
The help command is your gateway to understanding how to play the game, providing detailed information about any command, game system, or topic. Type 'help' alone for a quick overview of common commands, 'help [command name]' to get detailed usage instructions for a specific command, or 'help systems' to see major game systems like combat, navigation, or communication. You can also explore specific systems with 'help system [name]' to understand how major features work.

When you look up a command, you'll see its purpose, syntax, available aliases, and practical examples showing how to use it. Staff members see additional implementation details like source code locations and technical notes. If you search for something that doesn't exist, the help system will suggest similar topics and can even activate an AI-powered autohelper that searches the knowledge base to answer your question. This makes help incredibly flexible - you can ask direct questions ending with '?' and get contextual assistance. The help database includes both auto-synced command documentation and custom help topics, creating a comprehensive reference system that grows with the game.
helpless:
Voluntarily make yourself helpless, allowing others to perform restraint actions on you like tying, gagging, searching, or moving you. This is a roleplay consent command that puts you in a vulnerable state. When helpless, you can be restrained by other characters without needing to be knocked out in combat first. Use 'helpless on' to become helpless or 'helpless off' to return to normal.

This command is part of the prisoner/restraint system and is disabled in past timelines. When you become helpless, the action is announced to everyone in the room. This command is typically used for consensual restraint roleplay scenarios. The helpless state persists until you manually disable it or certain conditions change. Examples: 'helpless', 'helpless on', 'helpless off'.
helpsearch:
The helpsearch command searches through all help files and command documentation to find topics matching your keywords. Type 'helpsearch' followed by your search term, like 'helpsearch combat' or 'helpsearch inventory'. The system searches command names, summaries, and descriptions to find relevant matches, returning up to 15 results with brief snippets. You can optionally filter by category by adding a category name at the end, like 'helpsearch attack navigation'.

Each result shows the command or topic name, its category tag, and a brief description. If no matches are found, the system suggests different keywords or tells you to try 'commands' to see everything. After getting search results, use 'help <topic>' to read the full documentation for that specific command. This is particularly useful when you remember roughly what you want to do but don't remember the exact command name - search for concepts like 'fighting', 'talking', or 'movement' to find related commands.
hidedesc:
The hidedesc command conceals the detailed description of an item from other players, making it mysterious and private while still showing the item's name. When you hide an item's description, others can see that you have the item, but they can't see its detailed description, properties, or any special text about it until you reveal it with 'showdesc'.

This is perfect for keeping secrets - hidden tattoos under clothing, disguised weapons, mysterious objects you don't want examined, or items with backstories you want to reveal later. The command works on any item you own (carried, worn, or stored), and the hidden status persists until you choose to reveal it. Others see the item exists but get no details beyond the basic name, creating intrigue and allowing you to control when and how you reveal special items.
hold:
The hold command lets you take an item from your inventory or holster and hold it visibly in your hand, making it immediately ready for use. This is essential for weapons, tools, and any items you want others to see you're actively wielding. Using aliases like 'draw', 'unsheathe', or 'brandish' adds dramatic flair to your actions, and these verb choices are reflected in what others see.

Items you hold are displayed prominently when others look at you, and many commands (especially combat actions) require you to be holding the relevant item. If the item is stored in a holster on your belt or clothing, the hold command will automatically draw it with an appropriate message describing you pulling the weapon from its sheath. If you don't specify what to hold, the command shows you a menu of everything currently in your inventory or holstered, including items that are carried versus holstered so you know where they are.
home:
The home command instantly teleports you to your designated home location, which you set using the 'sethome' command in a room you own. This is a convenience feature for quickly returning to your private property, apartment, or personal space without having to walk or navigate there. When you use the command, people in your current location see you head home, and people at your home see you arrive.

You must own a room and have set it as your home before this command works - if you haven't, the game will prompt you to use 'sethome' first. You can't teleport home while in combat, as that would be using the command to escape danger. If you're already at your home location, the command tells you so rather than doing nothing. This command is perfect for ending roleplay sessions by returning to your private space, or quickly accessing your home base when you need to drop off items or rest between adventures.
in:
Move inward or inside to an adjacent interior room. This is a directional movement command specifically for entering interior spaces from exterior areas or moving deeper into a structure. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat.

This command works the same way as other direction commands (north, south, up, down, etc.) but specifically handles the 'in' direction for moving into interior spaces. Aliases include 'enter' and 'inside'. Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names. Example: 'in' or 'enter'.
inventory:
The inventory command shows you everything you're currently carrying, wearing, or holding. It provides a comprehensive view organized into logical sections: your wallet balances (showing money in various currencies), items currently held in your hands, items being carried in pockets or bags, and items you're wearing on your body.

Each item is displayed with helpful details like quantity (for stackable items), condition (if not in good shape), and damage level (if torn or broken). This command is essential for managing your possessions and checking what resources you have available.
