The display clearly separates what's immediately accessible in your hands versus what's stored away in your inventory versus what's being worn, making it easy to see what you need to retrieve or equip before use.
join channel:
Join a public communication channel to participate in group chat. Channels are persistent chat rooms that let you communicate with other players across the game world, regardless of your physical location. You can only join public channels - private channels require an invitation. When you join, all online members of the channel are notified of your arrival.

Once you're a member of a channel, use 'channel <name> <message>' to chat in it. Use the 'channels' command to see a list of available channels. You'll be notified when others join or leave channels you're in. Examples: 'join channel OOC', 'joinchannel General'.
journey:
Plan and manage world travel between major locations, including standard travel, flashback time travel, and party-based group journeys. This is the primary command for long-distance travel across the game world. Without arguments, it opens the travel map GUI or shows your current journey status. Use 'journey to <destination>' to start planning a trip, which presents options for travel mode (standard or various flashback modes).

The command supports multiple travel modes: standard (real-time travel with ETA), flashback instant (uses flashback time for instant arrival), flashback return (instant arrival with time reserved for return trip), and backloaded (instant arrival but accumulates return debt). You can also create group travel parties with 'journey invite <name>', check party status with 'journey party', and launch when ready with 'journey launch'. While traveling, use 'journey passengers' to see who's with you, 'journey disembark' to leave early (drops you in wilderness), and 'journey return' to end flashback instances. The system handles complex scenarios like travel parties, vehicle-based travel, and flashback time management.
knock:
Knock on a door or exit to alert people in another room that you're there. This is a roleplaying command that lets you announce your presence before entering someone's property or private space. Specify the direction or name of the exit you want to knock on. The sound of knocking is heard by all characters in the target room and throughout that property (if it's a multi-room property like an apartment).

Everyone in your current room also sees you knocking. This is more polite than just barging in, especially for private residences or closed doors. The command uses spatial adjacency to find valid exits to knock on. Examples: 'knock north', 'knock bedroom', 'knock door'.
landmarks:
The landmarks command displays a directory of public places in your current zone - all the rooms without private owners that anyone can visit. When you type 'landmarks', you see locations organized by area (villages, neighborhoods, districts) with each room tagged by type: [Shop], [Bank], [Park], [Street], [Building], etc. This helps you quickly identify what kind of places are available and where they're located in your area.

The list includes only public spaces, not private homes or restricted areas, making it a practical guide to community areas. After seeing the list, you can use 'walk <place name>' to navigate to any of these locations. This is particularly helpful for new players learning the geography of their starting zone or for anyone looking for specific public facilities like banks, shops, or parks. The command shows aliases like 'public', 'locations', or 'destinations', all of which work identically. Think of it as a tourist guide to publicly accessible spaces in your region.
lead:
The lead command works in two different ways depending on whether you're interacting with player characters or NPCs. When used on another player, 'lead <name>' grants them permission to follow you - they'll then be able to use the 'follow' command to trail along as you move. You can revoke this permission with 'lead stop <name>'. Typing 'lead' alone shows who's currently following you, helping you keep track of your group during travel or events.

When used on NPCs, lead works differently - you're asking the NPC to follow you rather than giving permission. The NPC uses AI to decide whether to accept based on their personality, your relationship, and the situation. They might agree immediately, ask questions, or refuse if they're suspicious or busy. NPCs can only follow one person at a time and have cooldown periods if they decline, so you'll need to wait before asking again. This creates dynamic interactions where building rapport with NPCs affects whether they'll accompany you on adventures or missions.
leave channel:
Leave a communication channel that you're currently a member of. When you leave a channel, you stop receiving messages from it and all online members are notified that you've left. You must be a member of the channel to leave it. Use the 'channels' command to see which channels you're currently in.

This is useful for managing channel clutter or leaving channels you're no longer interested in. You can always rejoin public channels later using 'join channel'. Examples: 'leave channel OOC', 'leavechannel General'.
leave event:
The leave event command exits the current event you're participating in and returns you to the normal room environment. When you leave, everyone still inside the event sees a departure message, and people in the room outside the event see you leave the event space. This cleanly ends your participation without physically moving you to a different location.

Events create a bubble of interaction - when you're inside one, you primarily see and interact with other event participants. Leaving ends that bubble and returns you to the regular room where you can interact with non-event attendees normally. You might leave early if you need to handle other matters, if the event is winding down, or if you simply want to rejoin the broader room. The event continues for others who remain inside. Use 'enter event' again if you change your mind and want to rejoin (assuming you haven't been bounced).
library delete:
Delete an item from your personal library, which can include saved media files (gradients, backgrounds, videos) or saved location bookmarks. The command searches both your media library and saved locations to find a match by name. When found, the item is permanently deleted from your library.

This is useful for cleaning up your library and removing items you no longer need. Be careful - deletion is permanent and cannot be undone. The command tells you what type of item was deleted (media type or location) for confirmation. Examples: 'library delete sunset', 'libdelete old gradient'.
lie:
The lie command lets you assume a prone position, either on the ground or on a specific piece of furniture like a bed or couch. This posture change affects your stance and is visible to others - when someone looks at you, they'll see that you're lying down and where you're positioned. Use 'lie down' to lie on the floor/ground, or 'lie on [furniture]' to use a specific piece of furniture.

Lying down restricts certain activities that require you to be standing (like moving to other rooms or engaging in combat) but is perfect for rest, sleep, injury roleplay, or casual lounging scenes. The command checks furniture capacity to ensure there's room, and your position is tracked so others can see you're occupying that furniture. This is one of several posture commands (along with sit and stand) that let you fully express your character's physical positioning and state in the game world.
listscenes:
View a list of all arranged scenes in the game (staff only). Arranged scenes are pre-planned roleplay encounters between NPCs and PCs that can be triggered when certain conditions are met. This command shows scene details including the NPC and PC involved, meeting location, roleplay room, status (pending/active/completed/cancelled), and any special conditions like availability windows.

You can filter the list by status: 'listscenes pending' (default, shows ready-to-trigger scenes), 'listscenes active' (currently running), 'listscenes completed' (finished scenes with summaries), 'listscenes cancelled', or 'listscenes all' (shows last 50). The command also displays which scenes are ready to trigger, expired, or have future availability dates. Use this to monitor and manage the arranged scene system. Staff can use 'sceneinstructions <id>' to set NPC behavior or 'cancelscene <id>' to cancel pending scenes.
look:
The look command is your window into the game world, allowing you to examine your surroundings, other characters, objects, and features in rich detail. Without any arguments, it shows you a complete view of your current location including its description, exits, people present, objects on the ground, and furniture or decorations.

You can look at specific things by adding a target: 'look [person]' to see someone's appearance and clothing, 'look [object]' to examine items, 'look [direction]' to peek at what's through an exit, or 'look [furniture]' to inspect decorative elements. The command is smart about finding what you're looking for, matching partial names and even understanding possessive forms like 'look Bob's sword' to see items worn or held by others. If you're blindfolded, your perception becomes limited to sounds and presence, while special modes like accessibility can transform the output into more structured, screen-reader-friendly text. When traveling by vehicle or on a journey, the look command adapts to show you your current mode of transport and surrounding terrain.
mail:
Read and send memos (mail) to other characters. This is the primary asynchronous communication system in the game. Use 'mail' alone to see your inbox with a quickmenu showing all messages. You can read specific memos by number, compose new messages using a form interface, and delete old memos. The system includes abuse detection, recent contact tracking for autocomplete, and Discord notifications for recipients.

In medieval/gaslight eras, memos are delivered by messenger service with realistic delays. In modern+ eras, delivery is instant. Commands: 'mail' (view inbox), 'mail read <num>' (read message), 'mail send <name>' (compose - can use shorthand: 'mail send Alice Meeting=See you at 5'), 'mail delete <num>' (delete specific), 'mail delete all' (clear inbox). The composer form includes autocomplete for recent contacts and validates message length. Self-memos work as personal notes.
make:
Create meta-structures and organizational elements (not physical items - use 'design' for that). This command creates abstract game elements like events, societies, memos, scenes, and room designations. Available types: 'make event' (calendar event - redirects to web), 'make society' (social groups/clubs - redirects to web), 'make memo <text>' (quick self-note), 'make scene [name]' (start roleplay scene), 'make entrance' (mark room as property entrance), 'make library' (designate arcane library for magic users).

The command serves as a hub for creating non-physical game elements. Some types redirect to web interfaces for complex creation (events, societies) while others work directly in-game (memos, scenes). Building-related types (entrance, library) require room ownership and affect room functionality.
make home:
The make home command (also 'makehome' or 'sethome') designates your current room as your home location, which you can then teleport back to using the 'home' command. You must own the room to set it as your home - you can't make someone else's property or a public space your home. When you set a new home, it replaces any previous home you had set. The system tells you if you're replacing an old home so you're aware of the change.

This is typically one of the first things you do after acquiring property - setting your apartment, house, or personal space as home gives you a quick way to return there from anywhere in the game world. You can only have one home at a time, so if you own multiple properties, choose the one you want as your primary return point. The 'home' command provides instant teleportation to this location (as long as you're not in combat), making it essential for convenience. Think of it like setting your spawn point or recall location in other games.
makeup:
Apply makeup to yourself or another character using the description editor. This opens a rich-text form where you can describe makeup applied to specific face areas (forehead, eyes, nose, cheeks, chin, mouth/lips). You can select multiple face areas if the makeup spans multiple regions, add optional reference images, and set display order for how the makeup appears in descriptions.

You need permission to apply makeup to other characters (either explicit permission or they must have granted you aesthete access). The makeup is stored as character descriptions and becomes part of how the character appears when looked at. You can specify which face area to start with: 'makeup me eyes' pre-selects eyes in the editor. Examples: 'makeup me', 'makeup me eyes', 'makeup Alice lips'.
map:
View various maps of your surroundings at different scales. Without arguments, presents a quickmenu with map options: room map (interior floorplan showing furniture and characters), area map (surrounding hex terrain), city map (zone overview with your location marked), and minimap toggle (persistent corner map). Maps are rendered dynamically based on your current location and cannot be viewed while blindfolded.

Room maps show furniture, other characters, exits, and your position using a canvas rendering system. Area/zone maps display hex-based terrain using world coordinates. City maps show an overview with a marker at your current position. The minimap option toggles a persistent corner map display. Examples: 'map' (show menu), 'map room', 'map area', 'map city', 'map mini' (toggle persistent minimap).
media:
Control media playback, screen sharing, and playlists in your current location. This is a comprehensive media system supporting YouTube watch parties, screen sharing, browser tab sharing with audio, and jukebox/playlist management. The command provides different options based on context: if there's an active media session you can control playback (play/pause/stop/seek), if there's a jukebox you can manage playlists, and you can always start screen/tab sharing.

Major features: YouTube watch parties with synchronized playback (host controls: 'media play', 'media pause', 'media seek 1:30'), screen sharing ('media share screen'), browser tab sharing with audio ('media share tab'), jukebox control ('media player play/stop/shuffle/loop'), and playlist management ('media playlist add <url>', 'media playlist remove <num>', 'media playlist list', 'media playlist clear'). Only the session host can control playback. The command can be invoked using various aliases: 'media', 'player', 'jukebox', 'playlist', 'share'. Without arguments, shows a contextual menu of available options.
meetup:
The meetup command uses automatic activity tracking to help you coordinate with other players by finding the best times when everyone is likely to be online together. Type 'meetup' followed by two or more character names (like 'meetup Alice Bob Carol') to see an analysis of when those characters typically play. The system examines historical activity patterns and shows you the top time slots when the maximum number of requested players are usually available, including which specific characters are active during each window.

You can also use 'meetup me' to see your own activity schedule - when you typically play, your peak times, and weekly patterns. This helps you understand your own habits and communicate your availability to others. The system requires sufficient data before showing patterns (at least 20 activity samples), so newer characters may see a message about insufficient data. All tracking is automatic and passive - you don't need to do anything special, just play normally. Privacy-conscious players can disable schedule sharing in their settings to prevent others from seeing overlap data. This command is invaluable for organizing roleplay scenes, coordinating group activities, or just finding when friends are most likely to be around.
msg:
Send a direct message (DM) to another player anywhere in the game world, regardless of their location. This is instant person-to-person communication that doesn't require being in the same room. The message is delivered immediately to the recipient and works across any distance. In modern eras with visible phone use, observers in your room may see you glance at your phone.

The command routes messages through DirectMessageService which handles era-appropriate delivery (instant in modern settings, may have delays in historical eras). Your last 10 DM contacts are tracked for status bar display. The target must be online and reachable. Examples: 'msg Alice Hey, where are you?', 'dm Bob Meeting at 5', 'text Charlie On my way!'. Aliases include 'dm' and 'text'.
narrate:
Toggle and control text-to-speech (TTS) narration of game content. This command provides comprehensive audio narration with playback controls, content filtering, and queue management. Use 'narrate' alone to toggle TTS on/off, or use subcommands for fine-grained control. You can configure which types of content get narrated (speech, actions, rooms, system messages), control playback (pause/resume/skip), and manage the audio queue.

The TTS system supports multiple voices for your character and narrator, with adjustable pitch and speed. Commands: 'narrate on/off' (enable/disable), 'narrate config speech on/off' (filter content types), 'narrate pause/resume' (playback control), 'narrate skip +15' (skip forward/backward seconds), 'narrate current' (jump to latest content), 'narrate clear' (clear queue), 'narrate queue' (view pending items), 'narrate status' (view full configuration). The service requires TTS availability from the system.
news:
The news command displays staff announcements and bulletins in different categories. When you type 'news' alone, it shows an overview of all categories with unread counts: Announcements (general game news), IC News (in-character world events), and OOC News (out-of-character player information). To read articles in a specific category, use 'news announcement', 'news ic', or 'news ooc'. Each article listing shows whether you've read it yet with a [NEW] marker.

To read a specific article, use 'news' followed by the article number, like 'news 5' or 'news read 5'. The system automatically marks articles as read when you view them, so your unread count stays accurate. This is how staff communicate important updates about game systems, upcoming events, world developments, or policy changes. Checking news regularly keeps you informed about what's happening in the game community and any changes that might affect your character or gameplay.
north:
Move in the specified cardinal direction to an adjacent room. This is one of the basic movement commands that lets you navigate through the game world. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat - use fight commands instead.

Each direction command (north, south, east, west, up, down, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest) works the same way. Just type the direction name or its abbreviation (n, s, e, w, u, d, ne, nw, se, sw). Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names.
northeast:
Move in the specified cardinal direction to an adjacent room. This is one of the basic movement commands that lets you navigate through the game world. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat - use fight commands instead.

Each direction command (north, south, east, west, up, down, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest) works the same way. Just type the direction name or its abbreviation (n, s, e, w, u, d, ne, nw, se, sw). Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names.
northwest:
Move in the specified cardinal direction to an adjacent room. This is one of the basic movement commands that lets you navigate through the game world. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat - use fight commands instead.

Each direction command (north, south, east, west, up, down, northeast, northwest, southeast, southwest) works the same way. Just type the direction name or its abbreviation (n, s, e, w, u, d, ne, nw, se, sw). Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names.
npc:
Manage NPC spawn locations and schedules (requires building permissions). This command lets you configure where and when NPCs appear in the game world. You can add the current room as an NPC schedule location, save rooms to your location library for future use, and manage your saved locations. The system supports detailed scheduling with time windows, day patterns, spawn probability, and activity descriptions.

Commands: 'npc location add' (add current room as spawn point with form for schedule details), 'npc location save <name>' (save current room to your location library), 'npc location list' (view saved locations), 'npc location del <name>' (remove from library). Shortcuts available: 'npc add', 'npc list', 'npc save', 'npc del'. When adding a location, you'll select an NPC archetype and configure schedule parameters: activity description, time window (start/end hours), active days (weekdays/weekends/all), and spawn probability percentage. Admins and users with NPC management permissions have access to all archetypes; others only see their own.
npcquery:
Ask any question of an NPC and receive their AI-generated response (staff only). This is a powerful debugging and content exploration tool that lets staff directly query NPCs using the NPC Leadership AI system. The command sends your question to the NPC's AI brain and returns their response, including their current location context. This bypasses normal conversation mechanics and provides immediate answers.

The NPC will respond based on their personality, memories, knowledge, and current situation. This is useful for understanding NPC behavior, testing dialogue systems, debugging quests, and exploring NPC knowledge without having to interact with them through normal gameplay. Format: 'npcquery <npc name> = <question>'. Examples: 'npcquery Bob = What do you think of the mayor?', 'asknpc Guard = What are you guarding?'. The command searches for NPCs globally and works whether they're in your room or not.
observe:
The observe command lets you focus your attention on a specific character, place, or the entire room to receive continuous updates about what's happening there. When you observe someone, you'll see detailed information about them including their physical appearance, clothing, held items, and any actions they take. You can observe other characters ('observe Alice'), yourself ('observe me'), a specific place like furniture ('observe bar'), or the whole room ('observe room').

While observing, you'll automatically see updates when your target moves, changes equipment, or performs actions - you don't need to keep typing 'look' repeatedly. This is particularly useful for security guards watching an entrance, doctors monitoring a patient, or simply when you want to stay aware of someone's activities during a scene. To stop observing, type 'observe stop' or just 'observe' with no target. You can only observe one thing at a time, and starting a new observation automatically ends the previous one.
ooc:
The ooc command sends private out-of-character messages directly to other players, completely separate from the in-character game world. This is your tool for player-to-player communication when you need to discuss real-world topics, coordinate scenes, ask questions, or just chat without breaking character immersion. Messages are delivered instantly regardless of where characters are located in the game.

You can message one person ('ooc Alice Hey there!') or multiple people at once using commas ('ooc Alice,Bob,Charlie Meeting at 8pm?'). The system tracks your recent OOC contacts, so typing 'ooc' without arguments shows you a list of people you've messaged recently for quick reference. Messages include timestamps and sender identification, and they're stored separately from in-character roleplay logs. This makes OOC perfect for planning scenes, discussing plot ideas, asking if someone is available to roleplay, or any player-to-player coordination that shouldn't happen in-character.
oocrequest:
Send an out-of-character (OOC) contact request to a player who requires permission before accepting OOC messages. This command is used when you want to contact someone OOC but they have their privacy settings set to require approval first. The request includes a message explaining why you want OOC contact. The target receives a quickmenu to accept or decline your request.

The system enforces cooldowns between requests to prevent spam. If the player has already granted you OOC permission, you should use 'pm' directly. If they've blocked you, the command will inform you. Format: 'oocrequest <character> <message>'. Example: 'oocrequest Bob Hi, would you like to discuss the plot?' The target is notified immediately if online and can respond with accept/decline options.
open roof:
Open the convertible roof of your vehicle. This command only works if you're currently in a vehicle that has a convertible roof feature. The roof state affects how you and other occupants experience the environment (weather, visibility, etc.). The action is announced to all passengers in the vehicle.

You must be inside the vehicle to use this command. If the vehicle isn't convertible or the roof is already open, you'll receive an error message. Pair with 'close roof' to control the roof state. Example: 'open roof'.
out:
Move outward or outside to an adjacent exterior room. This is a directional movement command specifically for leaving interior spaces and moving to exterior areas. The command checks if there's a passable room in that direction and moves you there if possible. You cannot use directional movement while in combat.

This command works the same way as other direction commands (north, south, up, down, etc.) but specifically handles the 'out' direction for exiting interior spaces. Aliases include 'exit', 'outside', and 'leave'. Movement is announced to both your current room and destination room with personalized names. Example: 'out' or 'exit'.
outfit:
Manage your saved clothing outfits and wardrobe. This command lets you save combinations of items you're wearing as named outfits, then quickly change into them later. Outfits have a class system that determines what gets removed when you wear them: 'full' removes everything, 'top' removes only tops, 'bottoms' removes only bottoms, etc. This prevents you from accidentally removing jewelry when changing shirts.

Commands: 'outfit' or 'outfit list' (view saved outfits), 'outfit save <name> [class]' (save current look - defaults to full class), 'outfit wear <name>' (change into outfit), 'outfit delete <name>' (remove saved outfit). Available classes: full (removes all worn items), top, bottoms, overwear, underwear, jewelry, accessories, other (removes nothing). When you wear an outfit, the system removes items based on the outfit class, then applies the saved items. Examples: 'outfit save Casual', 'outfit save Formal top', 'outfit wear Casual', 'outfit delete Old'.
pemit:
Send a private emit to specific character(s) in your room (staff only). This is a powerful storytelling tool that lets staff send narrative messages that only certain players can see. Unlike regular emits that everyone sees, pemits are private and appear in a distinct fieldset format to the targets. You can send to multiple targets at once by separating names with commas.

The command requires staff permissions and only works on characters in your current room. Format: 'pemit <target(s)> = <message>'. Examples: 'pemit John = A chill runs down your spine.', 'pemit Bob, Alice = You both feel a strange presence.' The targets see the emit wrapped in a fieldset for visual distinction, and you receive confirmation of who saw it. Use this for personalized narrative elements, secret information, or selective environmental descriptions.
pemote:
Make a puppeted NPC perform an emote (staff only). This command lets staff control NPCs they're puppeting by making them emote. The emote is broadcast to everyone in the NPC's room and logged for roleplay records. If you're puppeting multiple NPCs, use the format 'pemote <npc> = <emote>' to specify which one. If only puppeting one NPC, you can omit the name.

The system automatically prepends the NPC's name to the emote text unless it already starts with their name. Punctuation is added automatically if missing. You must have an active puppet session using the 'puppet' command first. Format: 'pemote <emote text>' (if puppeting one NPC) or 'pemote <npc name> = <emote text>' (if puppeting multiple). Examples: 'pemote waves hello', 'pemote Bob = smiles warmly'. The action is logged and you receive confirmation showing what happened and where.
permissions:
The permissions command is your comprehensive control panel for privacy, consent, and social boundaries. Typing 'permissions' alone opens a menu with three main areas: General Settings (default permissions for all players), Blocked Players (manage who you've blocked), and Content Consent (which mature/adult content types you allow). You can set permissions for specific individuals using 'permissions <name>', controlling things like who can see your location, send OOC messages, whisper to you IC, follow/lead you, dress/style you, and whether you see their channel messages.

The blocking system has granular controls - block someone entirely or just block specific interaction types like DMs, OOC messages, channels, physical interaction, or perception (they can't see you). Content consent settings determine what mature themes you're comfortable with in roleplay, with mutual consent required for sensitive content. You can set per-player overrides allowing specific content with trusted partners. Additional features include 'permissions unfriend' to reset relationship status without blocking, 'permissions blocks' to manage your block list, and 'permissions consent room' to see what content all current occupants mutually consent to. This system ensures everyone has agency over their roleplay experience and boundaries are respected.
pierce:
Pierce a body position (ear, nose, lip, etc.) using piercing jewelry from your inventory. You can add new piercings or change existing ones. When you pierce yourself, you'll wear the jewelry item in that position and other characters in the room will see what you're doing. The command requires you to have a piercing item in your inventory and specify which body position you want to pierce.

Use 'pierce <position> with <item>' to add or change a piercing. For example: 'pierce left ear with silver stud' or 'pierce nose with gold ring'. The jewelry must be in your inventory and flagged as a piercing type item.
places:
The places command shows you all the notable locations and furniture in your current room where you can sit, stand, or position yourself. When you use this command, you'll see a list of available places like chairs, tables, beds, bars, stages, or other points of interest. Each place shows its name and may display how many people can fit there (capacity) and who's currently present.

This is helpful for social roleplay - you can see where others are gathered and join them using commands like 'sit at bar' or 'stand at stage'. Some rooms have no special places and just have open floor space, in which case the command will tell you there are no notable places. The places shown are context-appropriate: a tavern might have a bar and tables, while a bedroom might have a bed and desk.
play:
Play YouTube videos in your current room so everyone present can watch together. Simply use 'play <YouTube URL>' to start a video. The video will appear in the room for all characters to see. You can also use the --sync flag to create a synchronized watch party where everyone's video stays in perfect sync as it plays.

Use 'play https://youtube.com/watch?v=...' for standard playback, or 'play --sync https://youtube.com/watch?v=...' to create a synchronized viewing session. This is great for watching movies, music videos, or tutorials together with other players in your location.
pocket:
The pocket command puts away items you're currently holding in your hands, returning them to your carried inventory. When you pocket something, it disappears from your visible equipped items and goes into your general inventory storage. The command is smart about holsters - if you're holding a weapon and wearing a compatible holster (like a sword belt or shoulder holster), it will automatically sheathe the weapon in the holster instead of just pocketing it.

You can use various aliases for flavor: 'stow', 'stash', 'put away', 'sheathe', and 'holster' all work and add roleplay variety to how you describe putting things away. The command supports 'pocket all' to quickly stow everything you're holding at once, which is convenient when you need your hands free. Others in the room see appropriate action messages describing you putting items away or sheathing weapons, making this a visible and immersive part of equipment management.
preview:
Preview items for sale in shops before you buy them. This command shows you the item's full description, price, stock availability, category, and type. It's useful when you want to see exactly what something looks like or read its details before spending your money.

Use 'preview <item>' when you're in a shop to see information about any item for sale. For example: 'preview sword' or 'preview leather jacket'. The command will find matching items in the shop's inventory and show you everything you need to know to make an informed purchase.
private:
The private command works in two different ways. When used alone ('private'), it toggles private mode on or off - a status that reveals adult descriptions and content when you look at characters who are also in private mode. The room sees you enter or leave private mode. This mode exists to ensure mature content is only visible to consenting adults who've explicitly opted in, providing a layer of consent beyond the content consent system.

When used with a target and action ('private Bob winks knowingly' or 'private to Alice makes a subtle gesture'), it performs a private emote that only you and your target can see - completely invisible to other people in the room. This creates intimate moments or secret signals between characters without broadcasting to observers. The sender sees '(Private to Bob) Alice winks knowingly' while the target sees '(Privately) Alice winks knowingly at you' with their name replaced by 'you'. This is perfect for clandestine signals, intimate gestures, or any interaction you want to keep between just two characters even in a crowded room. The system prevents spam and validates content to prevent abuse.
profile:
The profile command displays a character's profile sheet with their identity, physical attributes, and current status. When you type 'profile' alone, you see your own profile. When you include a character name like 'profile Alice', you view that character's public profile information. Profiles show the character's full name, race, class, gender, age, height, ethnicity, and any profile picture they've uploaded. There's also a short description section where players write a quick summary of their character concept.

The profile includes real-time status information: whether the character is currently online or offline, what room they're in, their current posture (sitting, standing, lying), and what furniture they're using if applicable. You'll also see their character level. This command is perfect for getting to know other characters at a glance, refreshing your memory about someone, or checking if a friend is online and where they might be found. Unlike 'look', which shows physical appearance during roleplay, profile gives you meta-information about the character as a whole.
property:
Manage your property ownership and access control for rooms and doors. This powerful command lets you lock or unlock rooms and doors you own, grant or revoke access to specific characters, and view lists of your properties and who has access to them. You can control who can enter your private spaces and manage keys to your doors.

Common uses: 'property lock' to lock your current room, 'property unlock' to unlock it, 'property grant <character>' to give someone access, 'property revoke <character>' to remove their access, 'property list' to see all your properties, and 'property access' to see who has access to your current location. You can also manage door locks with 'property lock door <direction>' and grant temporary or permanent access rights.
puppet:
Take control of an NPC character to roleplay as them directly. This staff command lets you puppet (control) NPCs in your current room or search for them globally. Once you're puppeting an NPC, you can issue commands as that character, speak as them, move them around, and interact with players naturally. This is useful for running plots, responding to player actions, or creating dynamic storytelling moments.

Use 'puppet <NPC name>' to start controlling an NPC. The command will search your current room first, then expand globally if needed. While puppeting, you act as that character until you stop puppeting. This command is restricted to staff members only.
puppets:
View a list of all NPCs currently being puppeted by staff members. This command shows which NPCs are under direct staff control, where they are located, how long they've been puppeted, and any pending AI suggestions for those characters. It's useful for staff coordination to see who is controlling which NPCs and avoid conflicts.

Use 'puppets' to see the full list. The display includes the NPC's name, their current location, how long they've been puppeted, and whether they have any queued AI behavior suggestions waiting. This command also provides quick reference information about puppet control commands.
quiet:
Toggle quiet mode to hide all channel messages from OOC, global, area, and group channels. This is useful when you want to focus on in-character roleplay without distractions from out-of-character chatter. When you enable quiet mode, other characters will see a message that you've put on headphones and tuned out. When you disable it, you'll be offered a catch-up summary if you missed any messages while you were in quiet mode.

Use 'quiet' to turn it on, then use 'quiet' again to turn it off. If you missed messages while in quiet mode, you'll get a quickmenu asking if you want to review up to 100 of them before fully exiting quiet mode.
quit:
The quit command safely logs your character out of the game and ends your play session. When you quit, your character 'goes to sleep' in their current location, and other players in the room see a departure message. The system automatically handles cleanup: it stops any active observations, ends following if you're trailing another character, clears AFK status, and records your total playtime for the session. Your character's state is saved, so when you return, you'll be exactly where you left off.

You cannot quit while actively fighting in combat - you must finish or flee from the battle first. This prevents using logout as an escape mechanism during dangerous situations. The command works with several aliases including 'logout', 'sleep', and 'log out', all of which do exactly the same thing. When you're ready to return to the game, simply log back in through the website and your character will wake up in the same location where they went to sleep.
redecorate:
Completely refresh a room's decorations by removing all existing decorative descriptions and replacing them with a single new one. This is perfect when you want to dramatically change a room's atmosphere or start fresh with a new vision, rather than adding to what's already there.

Unlike the 'decorate' command which adds to your existing decorations, 'redecorate' gives you a clean slate. If you've accumulated many decoration layers and want to simplify, or if you're completely reimagining a space, this command lets you sweep away the old and establish a new baseline in one action.
release:
Release a character that you are currently dragging or carrying. If you've been carrying someone (holding them), this puts them down gently in your current location. If you've been dragging someone behind you (like a prisoner or unconscious person), this releases your grip on them and they stop following you. Other characters in the room will see what you're doing, and the released character will be notified that you've let them go.

Use 'release', 'putdown', or 'letgo' to release whoever you're currently restraining. You must be either carrying or dragging someone for this command to work.
remove:
The remove command takes off clothing or jewelry that you're currently wearing and returns it to your inventory. You can remove a single item with 'remove shirt', or strip off multiple items at once with 'remove hat, scarf, gloves' or 'remove hat and scarf'. The command supports various verb aliases like 'doff' and 'take off' for added roleplay flavor.

When you successfully remove something, everyone in the room sees an action message describing what you did. If you use the command without specifying what to remove, you'll get a menu showing everything you're currently wearing, organized by type (clothing versus jewelry) to make it easy to select. This is particularly useful when you're wearing many items and want to browse what's on you before deciding what to take off.
rename:
The rename command changes the name of a room you own. Simply type 'rename' followed by the new name you want, like 'rename Cozy Living Room' or 'rename The Hidden Workshop'. The new name must be 100 characters or less and replaces the old name completely. Other players in the room see you renaming it, and the change takes effect immediately for everyone.

This is useful for personalizing your property, updating names as rooms get repurposed, or fixing names you're not happy with. You can rename rooms as many times as you want as long as you own them. You must have ownership of the outer room (the main container) to rename any rooms within it - renters or visitors cannot rename rooms they don't own. The command requires room modification permissions to work. This is one of the basic customization options for property owners who want their spaces to feel unique and properly labeled.
reputation:
View a player character's reputation information including their three reputation tiers (public knowledge, social circles, and close associates) and their top 10 linked world memories. This staff command is useful for understanding how a character is perceived in the game world and what notable events they've been involved in. The reputation tiers help staff and players understand what information about a character would be common knowledge versus private.

Use 'reputation <character name>' or 'rep <name>' to view a PC's reputation. The command shows all three reputation tiers, when they were last updated, and displays the character's most important world memories sorted by relevance score (a combination of importance and recency).
reroll:
The reroll command permanently deletes your character and sends you to character creation to start over with a fresh character. This is only available when your character is dead - you cannot use it to escape a living character you've grown tired of. When you reroll, your character 'fades from existence' with all their history, relationships, belongings, and progress permanently erased. There is no undo, backup, or way to recover a rerolled character.

This command exists for players who want to try a completely new character concept after their character has died, rather than resurrecting the same one. It's a clean slate option - useful if your character's story has reached a natural conclusion, if you want to explore different gameplay styles, or if you simply want a fresh start in the game world. The system logs you out after deletion and redirects you to character creation. Use 'quit' instead if you want to log out normally with a living character. Only use reroll if you're absolutely certain you want to permanently delete your character and start over.
resize room:
Change the physical dimensions of a room you own. You can specify width, depth, and height in feet to resize the room's boundaries. This is useful for customizing your property to better fit your needs or match your vision. If the room is nested inside another room, the new dimensions must fit within the parent room's boundaries. The room will remain centered at its current location while its size changes.

Use 'resize room <width> <depth> <height>' with all three dimensions in feet. For example: 'resize room 20 15 10' creates a room that's 20 feet wide, 15 feet deep, and 10 feet tall. Dimensions must be positive numbers and cannot exceed the maximum room size limit.
reskin:
Change the appearance of a stored item by applying a different pattern to it. This lets you customize your belongings without changing their fundamental properties - you're just giving them a new look. The item must be in your storage (not in your inventory) and must have a pattern associated with it. You can either view available patterns for an item or directly apply a specific pattern if you know its name.

Use 'reskin <item>' to see all available pattern options for that item, or 'reskin <item> to <pattern>' to directly apply a specific pattern. For example: 'reskin sword' shows available sword patterns, or 'reskin leather jacket to denim jacket' changes your jacket's appearance. Only stored items can be reskinned.
roll:
The roll command performs a dice roll based on your character's statistics, rolling 2d8 (with exploding 8s!) plus a modifier calculated from the stat(s) you choose. This is the core mechanic for resolving uncertain actions, skill checks, and contests in the game. When you roll, everyone in the room sees dramatic animated dice plus your total result, creating shared moments of tension and excitement.

You can roll a single stat like 'roll STR' for a pure strength check, or combine multiple stats with 'roll STR+DEX' for complex actions that draw on multiple abilities. Eights explode - when you roll an 8, you roll again and add to your total, potentially creating spectacular cascading results. If you don't specify a stat, you'll get a menu showing all your available stats with their current values. The roll command integrates with the Auto-GM system, so if a GM has requested a roll from you for a specific challenge or task, your roll will automatically be evaluated against their difficulty target and you'll be told whether you succeeded or failed.
roomtitle:
Set or clear the short status text that appears after your name in room descriptions. Your room title is a brief phrase—like 'looking thoughtful', 'leaning against the wall', or 'covered in mud'—that gives other players a quick sense of what your character is doing or how they appear at a glance. It's displayed right after your name whenever someone looks at the room or checks who's present.

Use 'roomtitle <text>' to set your title (up to 200 characters), 'roomtitle clear' to remove it, or just 'roomtitle' with no arguments to see your current title. Room titles are perfect for conveying mood, posture, or ongoing actions without writing a full emote. They persist until you change or clear them, so they're great for establishing a character's default demeanor or situation during a scene.
save:
The save command captures images or videos from recent chat messages and stores them in your personal media library with a custom name. When someone shares a picture or video link in chat, you can save it by typing 'save pic1 as sunset' (for the first image) or 'save vid2 as funny' (for the second video). The system searches your last 50 messages for media URLs, counts them by type, and saves the one you specified. You can then recall these saved items later from your library.

Supported media types include 'pic'/'picture'/'image' for photos, 'vid'/'video' for videos, and specialized types like 'tpic' (text-to-image) and 'tvid' (text-to-video). The command extracts URLs from various formats including direct links, markdown syntax, and embedded media from platforms like YouTube and Vimeo. Each saved item needs a unique name - if you try to use a name you've already used, the system tells you to delete the old one first with 'library delete <name>'. This feature is perfect for collecting reference images, saving funny memes shared in RP, or building a personal gallery of memorable moments and media from your gameplay.
save gradient:
Save a custom color gradient to your personal media library for later use. Gradients are defined as a series of hex color codes separated by commas, with at least two colors required. Once saved, you can reference this gradient by name when customizing items, profiles, or other visual elements. Each gradient must have a unique name in your library.

Use 'save gradient <hex codes> as <name>' with comma-separated colors. For example: 'save gradient #ff0000,#00ff00,#0000ff as rainbow' or 'savegrad #000000,#ffffff as monochrome'. Hex codes can be 3 or 6 characters (with or without the # prefix). If you already have something saved with that name, delete it first with 'library delete <name>'.
save location:
Bookmark your current location so you can easily reference or return to it later. This saves the room you're in along with a name of your choice, storing it in your personal library. Bookmarks are useful for remembering important places like your home, favorite shops, meeting spots, or secret locations you've discovered. The command also records what zone the location is in for easy reference.

Use 'save location as <name>', 'saveloc as <name>', or 'bookmark as <name>' to save your current room. For example: 'save location as home' or 'bookmark as secret hideout'. Each bookmark must have a unique name in your library - if you try to use a name that already exists, you'll need to delete the old bookmark first.
say:
The say command is your primary tool for in-character communication with others in the same room. It's incredibly versatile, supporting multiple speaking styles through verb aliases (yell, whisper, mutter, etc.), emotional modifiers through adverbs (quietly, excitedly, nervously), and even directed speech.

When you speak, your message is visible to everyone present and gets saved in the roleplay logs. You can add atmosphere to your speech by using adverbs like 'say quietly I have a secret' or verb variations like 'yell' for louder communication. For directed conversation, use 'say to [person] [message]' to speak directly to someone, or use 'reply [message]' to automatically respond to the last person who spoke to you. You can even speak through doorways or exits using 'say through [exit], [message]' to communicate with people in adjacent rooms. The command intelligently tracks conversation context, so if you're having an ongoing discussion with someone, you can often omit the target and it will know who you mean.
scene:
Begin an arranged scene meeting with an NPC at the designated location. When you've been invited to meet with an NPC (either through roleplay or a staff-organized event), this command triggers the meeting and brings the NPC into the scene with you. If you have multiple arranged meetings available at your location, you'll get a menu to choose which one to start. The command creates a roleplay environment for you to interact with the NPC.

Use 'scene', 'meet', or 'startscene' when you're in the right location for a scheduled NPC meeting. If there's no arranged scene waiting for you at your current location, you'll be told to go to the designated meeting place first. The command works automatically - just be in the right spot and use it.
sceneinstructions:
Set or update the behavioral instructions that an NPC will follow during an arranged scene. This staff command lets you give specific guidance to NPCs about what they should mention, how they should act, or what topics to focus on during a meeting with players. The instructions influence the NPC's AI-generated responses and actions. If the scene is already active and the NPC is online, the instructions are applied immediately; otherwise they'll take effect when the scene triggers.

Use 'sceneinstructions <scene_id> = <instructions>' to set NPC behavior. For example: 'sceneinstructions 5 = mention the weather and offer a discount' or 'sceneinstruct 12 = be suspicious and ask about their recent activities'. This only works for pending or active scenes - completed scenes cannot be modified.
score:
The score command displays a comprehensive character sheet showing all of your character's statistics, abilities, and current state. It presents your vital information like level, experience, health, and mana at a glance, followed by your primary and secondary attributes (strength, dexterity, intelligence, etc.), any learned skills, and a complete list of your combat abilities with their cooldown status.

The display is organized into logical sections for easy scanning - vital stats at the top, then primary attributes, secondary attributes, skills, and abilities. Any temporary modifiers to your stats (from buffs, injuries, or effects) are shown in parentheses so you can see both your base values and your current effective values. The command also shows your current stance, position (if you're at furniture), and location, giving you a complete snapshot of your character's mechanical and narrative state. This is essential for understanding your capabilities before engaging in combat, checking ability cooldowns, or simply reviewing your character's development progress.
search:
Search a helpless or restrained character to see what they're wearing and carrying. This command lets you examine another character's possessions, including their worn clothing and equipment, items in their inventory, and any money they have. The target must be helpless (unconscious, tied up, or otherwise unable to resist). Everyone in the room will see you searching them, and the target will be notified that you're going through their belongings.

Use 'search <character>', 'frisk <name>', or 'rob <name>' to search someone. For example: 'search Bob' or 'frisk the unconscious guard'. The results show everything they're wearing, everything they're carrying (with quantities), and all their money organized by currency type. You can't search yourself - use the 'inventory' command for that.
searchmemory:
Search world memories using semantic similarity to find events matching your query. This staff command uses AI embeddings to find memories that are conceptually related to your search terms, not just keyword matches. It's useful for finding past events, researching character histories, or tracking down specific incidents that players or NPCs were involved in. The search returns memories ranked by similarity score, importance, and recency.

Use 'searchmemory <query>', 'memsearch <query>', or 'searchmem <query>' with natural language. For example: 'searchmemory bandits attacking the village' or 'memsearch guild meeting about expansion plans'. The results show up to 10 matching memories with their similarity percentage, importance rating, and age. Use 'viewmemory <id>' to see the full details of any interesting match.
seed:
Inject a specific instruction into an NPC's next AI-generated action or dialogue. This staff command lets you guide NPC behavior by telling them what to mention, what to do, or how to respond in their next interaction. The instruction influences the LLM's output when the NPC takes their next action, helping you create specific story moments or guide roleplay in a particular direction. The command searches your current room first, then globally if needed.

Use 'seed <npc name> = <instruction>' to guide NPC behavior. For example: 'seed Bob = mention the weather and ask about the player's day' or 'seed merchant = offer a special discount on healing potions'. The instruction doesn't force exact words - it influences the NPC's AI-generated response while maintaining their personality. You can't seed instructions for NPCs being fully puppeted by other staff members.
semiafk:
Toggle semi-away (semi-AFK) status to indicate you're partially present but distracted. This is useful when you're multitasking, checking your phone, or otherwise not fully focused on the game. Other players in your room will see a status message that you're distracted. You can optionally specify how many minutes you'll be semi-away for, or leave it indefinite. The status clears automatically after the time expires, or you can toggle it off manually.

Use 'semiafk', 'semi-afk', 'semi', or 'distracted' to toggle the status. Add a number to set a duration: 'semiafk 30' means you'll be semi-away for 30 minutes. When you return to full focus, use the same command again to clear the status and let others know you're back.
semote:
Perform roleplay actions with automatic extraction and execution of game commands. This is a "smart emote" that uses AI to interpret your freeform narrative text and automatically execute relevant game commands in the background. For example, if you semote "stands up and walks to the door", the game will extract the implied actions (stand, walk to door) and execute them while broadcasting your emote. This lets you write natural, flowing roleplay without stopping to type individual commands.

Use 'semote <action>', 'smartemote <action>', or 'sem <action>' followed by your narrative text. The game processes your emote immediately so other players see it right away, then extracts and executes commands in the background. Works best with clear, actionable descriptions. Speech in your semote is colored using your character's speech color for visual clarity.
set background:
Set a background image for a room you own. This changes the visual backdrop that players see when they're in your room, letting you customize the atmosphere with images from the web. You must provide a direct URL to an image file. To remove a background, use the command without a URL. Other characters in the room will be notified when you change the background.

Use 'set background <url>', 'setbg <url>', or 'setbackground <url>' with a valid image URL. For example: 'set background https://example.com/room.jpg'. The URL must start with http:// or https:// and be under 2048 characters. To remove the background: 'set background' with no URL.
set seasonal:
Configure seasonal and time-of-day variations for room descriptions and backgrounds. This advanced room customization lets you set different text descriptions and background images that display automatically based on the time of day (morning, afternoon, evening, night) and season (spring, summer, fall, winter). You can create specific combinations like "winter morning" or use wildcards to apply settings to any time or season. This makes your room feel dynamic and responsive to the game world's changing conditions.

Use 'set seasonal desc <time> <season> <text>' for descriptions or 'set seasonal bg <time> <season> <url>' for backgrounds. Use '-' as a wildcard for either field. For example: 'set seasonal desc morning spring The spring sun rises...' or 'set seasonal bg - winter https://example.com/snow.jpg'. Use 'set seasonal list' to see all your current settings, and 'set seasonal clear <desc|bg> <time> <season>' to remove specific variations.
shop:
The shop command opens the interface for buying items from shops, browsing inventory, and (if you own the shop) managing stock and prices. When used in a room with a shop, it shows you a menu to browse items, make purchases, or access owner management options. The shop system supports both cash-only shops (wallet money only) and shops that can charge your bank account.

As a customer, you can 'shop buy [item]' to purchase things, with support for buying multiple quantities like 'shop buy 3 potion'. The system shows item prices, stock levels, and organizes items by category. If you own the shop (by owning the room it's in), you get additional options to add items from your inventory with 'shop add [price] [item]', remove items with 'shop remove [item]', and view current stock levels. Shop transactions are visible to others in the room, money is automatically deducted from your wallet and/or bank account, and purchased items go straight into your inventory. This makes shops great for both established merchant businesses and pop-up player-run stores.
show:
The show command lets you display an item from your inventory to another character, giving them a detailed look at it including its description, condition, and any special properties. When you show something to someone, they receive a private message with the full item details, while other people in the room see that you're showing something but don't see what it is or its details. Use it with 'show [item] to [person]' format.

This is perfect for sharing treasures, displaying evidence, presenting gifts before giving them, or showing off interesting items without having to drop them on the ground. The command automatically includes quantity, condition, and damage information in the display so the recipient gets complete details. If you don't specify what to show, you'll get a menu of your inventory with everything you're carrying and holding, organized by status, making it easy to select what you want to present.
showdesc:
The showdesc command reveals the detailed description of an item that you've previously hidden, making it visible again when others look at or examine the item. By default, item descriptions are visible, but if you've used 'hidedesc' to make an item's details private, this command reverses that and makes the description public again.

This is useful for items that you want to keep mysterious initially but later reveal - hidden tattoos that you uncover, concealed weapons that you brandish openly, or secret documents that you decide to share. The command works on any item you own whether it's in your inventory, worn on your body, or stored elsewhere. Once you show the description, anyone who looks at you or examines the item will see its full details again.
sit:
The sit command lets you take a seated position, either on the ground/floor or on a specific piece of furniture. You can use it simply as 'sit' to sit where you are, or target furniture with 'sit on couch', 'sit at table', or 'sit in booth'. The command supports many natural prepositions and even has verb variations like 'sprawl', 'lounge', and 'kneel' that add flavor to your pose.

When you sit on furniture, the system tracks your position and ensures there's enough room - each piece of furniture has a capacity limit. The preposition you use (on, at, in) is remembered and shown to others when they look at you. Many furniture items have a default preposition that fits their type (you sit 'at' a bar, 'in' a booth, 'on' a couch), but you can override this with your own choice. Note that being seated prevents certain actions that require you to be standing, so you may need to 'stand' before moving or engaging in combat.
smoke:
The smoke command lets you light up and smoke items like cigarettes, cigars, or pipes from your inventory, creating atmospheric roleplay moments with wisps of smoke and the social ritual of smoking. When you smoke something, the action is visible to others in the room with descriptive text about lighting up and smoke curling upward.

The smoking process happens over time, allowing for realistic scenes where characters share smokes, take breaks together, or use smoking as a character trait or social bonding moment. You can use aliases like 'puff' or 'light up' for variation. Smokeable items can have different properties and effects - some might be purely aesthetic for roleplay, while others could provide mechanical benefits, debuffs, or special flavor text describing the taste and experience of the smoke.
south:
Move south to an adjacent room. This is a quick directional movement command that instantly moves your character one room in the southern direction. The command only works if there's a valid exit to the south from your current location - if the way is blocked by walls, locked doors, or if no exit exists in that direction, you won't be able to move. Use the 'exits' command to see which directions are available from your current room.

Use 'south' or the shortcut 's' to move south. This is functionally equivalent to 'move south' or 'walk south' but faster to type. If you need to check where south leads before moving, use the 'look' command to see exit descriptions.
southeast:
Move southeast to an adjacent room. This diagonal movement command moves your character one room in the southeastern direction. The command only works if there's a valid exit to the southeast from your current location - if the way is blocked by walls, locked doors, or if no exit exists in that direction, you won't be able to move. Diagonal exits are less common than cardinal directions but are used in some outdoor areas and city grids.

Use 'southeast' or the shortcut 'se' to move southeast. This is functionally equivalent to 'move southeast' or 'walk southeast' but faster to type. Use the 'exits' command to see which directions are available from your current room, including diagonal ones.
southwest:
Move southwest to an adjacent room. This diagonal movement command moves your character one room in the southwestern direction. The command only works if there's a valid exit to the southwest from your current location - if the way is blocked by walls, locked doors, or if no exit exists in that direction, you won't be able to move. Diagonal exits are less common than cardinal directions but are used in some outdoor areas and city grids.

Use 'southwest' or the shortcut 'sw' to move southwest. This is functionally equivalent to 'move southwest' or 'walk southwest' but faster to type. Use the 'exits' command to see which directions are available from your current room, including diagonal ones.
spar:
The spar command challenges another character to a friendly sparring match, which works just like regular combat but tracks 'touches' instead of actual damage - no one gets hurt or loses HP. This is perfect for practice fights, training sessions, exhibitions, or competitive duels where you want the tactical excitement of combat without the risk of injury or death.

Sparring matches use the same combat system as real fights - you choose targets, select actions, use abilities, and navigate the battle map in rounds - but the outcome is measured in successful strikes scored rather than health lost. When you challenge someone to spar, they see the same combat quickmenu interface, and all the tactical positioning, attack rolls, and ability usage work identically to real combat. Observers see the action unfold with full combat narration, making sparring great for demonstrations, tournaments, or just showing off your fighting style in a safe, non-lethal context.
staffroom:
Instantly teleport to the staff room from anywhere in the game world. This staff-only command provides quick access to the staff coordination area where staff members can meet, discuss issues, and work on administrative tasks without traveling through the game world normally. When you use this command, you vanish from your current location and reappear in the staff room with magical teleportation effects visible to other characters.

Use 'staffroom', 'staff room', 'staff-room', or 'office' to teleport to the staff area. The command only works for staff members - players without staff privileges cannot access this teleportation. If you're already in the staff room, the command will tell you so instead of teleporting you nowhere.
stand:
The stand command lets you rise to a standing position from sitting or lying down. You can use it simply as 'stand' or 'stand up' to get up from wherever you are, or you can stand at a specific piece of furniture with 'stand at bar' or 'stand by window'. The command even supports fun variations like 'dance on stage' that use standing posture with creative descriptions.

When you stand at furniture, you maintain a connection to that object (visible when others look at you) while keeping your standing posture, which allows you to move around and participate in actions that require you to be upright. Standing is often required before you can walk to another room or engage in combat. If you're sitting on furniture and want to fully disconnect from it, just use 'stand' or 'stand up' without specifying a location to stand freely in the room.
start event:
Begin a scheduled event that you're organizing. This command transitions an event from scheduled status to active, allowing participants to join using 'enter event'. The event must be scheduled (not already active, completed, or cancelled) and you must be the organizer to start it. When you start an event, anyone at the event location is notified that it's beginning, and you're automatically entered as the host.

Use 'start event' if you have a single scheduled event at your current location, or 'start event <name>' to specify which event to start if you have multiple. For example: 'start event Birthday Party'. Only the event organizer can start their events - other characters cannot start events they don't own.
stop:
The stop command is a universal way to cancel various ongoing activities - it intelligently detects what you're doing and ends it. If you're in the middle of walking somewhere, stop halts your movement immediately. If you're following another character, it makes you stop trailing them. If you're observing someone or something, it ends that observation. If you're on a long world journey or voyage, it cancels the travel and brings you to your current location on the route.

You can be more specific by typing 'stop following', 'stop observing', or 'stop journey' to target exactly what you want to cancel, but usually just 'stop' is enough - the game figures out what makes sense. This is particularly useful in emergencies or when your plans change mid-action. The command also has a 'halt' alias that works identically. If you're not doing any of these activities, the game will tell you there's nothing to stop.
strip:
The strip command quickly removes all your worn clothing at once. To prevent accidents, you must type either 'strip all' or 'strip naked' - the command won't work with just 'strip' alone. When you strip, everyone in the room sees you removing your clothing. All removed items remain in your inventory, just unworn. This is useful when you need to change outfits completely or when preparing for activities like swimming. Use 'wear' to put clothing back on, or 'remove <item>' if you only want to take off specific pieces.

The command respects your character's privacy settings and won't override any consent boundaries. If you're wearing many items, this saves time compared to removing each piece individually with the 'remove' command.
style:
The style command opens a rich text editor where you can create or modify hairstyle descriptions for yourself or others. Unlike simpler commands that work with pre-existing items, style lets you write freeform descriptive text about hair - its length, color, cut, styling, and any accessories like ribbons or clips. The editor supports markdown formatting for emphasis, colors, and structure.

You can style your own hair with 'style me' or style someone else with 'style [person]' if you have permission (they must allow you to modify their appearance). The hairstyle description you create is saved as a character description that appears on the scalp body position, and it shows up when others look at you. You can optionally include an image URL and set a display order if you want to control how it appears relative to other descriptions. This is part of the broader aesthete system that allows detailed character customization.
subtle:
Perform a subtle roleplay action that's only fully visible to characters who are nearby or at the same place as you. This command is perfect for quiet gestures, discreet signals, secretive actions, or subtle movements that wouldn't be noticed across a large room. Characters at your table or place will see your full action, while characters elsewhere in the room will only see a vague indication that someone did something quietly. This creates realistic visibility rules for roleplay.

Use 'subtle <action>' with your action text. For example: 'subtle slides a note across the table', 'subtle winks conspiratorially at Bob', or 'subtle adjusts the hidden knife in her boot'. The command respects social groupings - if you're at a couch, only people at that couch see the details; others see something like "[Someone at the couch does something quietly.]"
summon:
Send a message to summon an NPC to your location. This command lets you call for NPCs you know, requesting they come to where you are. The NPC will receive your message and use their AI to decide whether to accept or decline the summons based on their personality, relationship with you, current activities, and the urgency of your request. If they accept, they'll travel to your location. If they decline, there's a cooldown period before you can summon them again.

Use 'summon <npc name> = <message>' with both the NPC's name and a message explaining why you need them. For example: 'summon Merchant = I need to buy supplies urgently' or 'call Guard = There's trouble at the tavern!' The NPC must be within summon range (known to you and reachable), cannot be currently with you, and must be marked as summonable. The message helps the NPC decide whether your request is worth responding to.
tattoo:
Open the tattoo editor to give yourself or someone else permanent body art. This command creates lasting tattoos on character bodies, storing them as permanent appearance descriptions. You can select one or multiple body positions for the tattoo (like arms, back, chest), write a detailed description with formatting and colors, optionally add an image URL, specify whether it's normally hidden by clothing, and control the display order among multiple tattoos. You need permission to tattoo someone else.

Use 'tattoo <target>' to open the editor, or 'tattoo <target> <body region>' to pre-select a region. For example: 'tattoo me' opens the editor to tattoo yourself, 'tattoo me back' pre-selects the back region, or 'tattoo Alice arm' opens the editor to tattoo Alice's arm (requires her permission). The form lets you pick multiple body positions, write rich descriptions using markdown, and configure visibility settings.
taxi:
The taxi command provides era-appropriate rapid transit within your zone, adapting its flavor to your game's time period - horse-drawn hansom cabs in Victorian settings, standard taxis in modern settings, rideshare vehicles in contemporary times, autocabs in near-future, or hover taxis in far-future scenarios. Type 'taxi' alone to see a menu of destination options with nearby public landmarks, or use 'taxi to <destination>' to travel directly to a named location. The system automatically handles calling a taxi if one isn't already present.

When you board, other players see an era-appropriate departure message (carriages clattering away, cars driving off, hover vehicles lifting into skylanes). Taxis only work in eras where such services exist - medieval settings won't have them. The command has many intuitive aliases including 'hail cab', 'call taxi', 'rideshare', 'uber', or 'autocab' depending on what feels natural for your setting. Taxi travel is quick and convenient compared to walking, perfect for crossing large zones rapidly or reaching distant landmarks. The service integrates with the game's timeline and era systems to maintain period-appropriate immersion.
think:
The think command lets you express your character's internal thoughts and mental state, showing what's going on inside their head. When you think, only you see the thought by default, but it's also visible to anyone actively observing you (like a staff member monitoring roleplay) and to characters with telepathic abilities who are reading your mind. This creates layered narrative depth - your public actions versus your private thoughts.

The command supports many verb aliases that change the flavor of your internal monologue: 'wonder' for curiosity, 'worry' for anxiety, 'ponder' for deep contemplation, 'hope' or 'wish' for desires, 'remember' for recollection, or 'feel' for emotions. Each verb adds nuance to how your thoughts are presented. Your thoughts are displayed in italics with quotes to distinguish them from spoken words, like '*Alice thinks, "I wonder what she meant by that"*'. While invisible to most, telepaths see your thoughts with a special [Telepathy] tag, making this command crucial for psychic powers and mind-reading abilities.
tickets:
Comprehensive ticket system for submitting and tracking bug reports, typos, behavior issues, feature requests, and suggestions. This command provides a full interface for managing your communication with staff about game issues or ideas. You can submit new tickets, view your open tickets, check the history of resolved tickets, and see detailed status and staff responses. Tickets automatically capture game context like your location and timestamp to help staff investigate.

Use 'tickets' to open the main menu, 'tickets list' to see open tickets, 'tickets new' to submit a ticket, or 'tickets view <id>' to see a specific ticket. Quick shortcuts: 'bug' for bug reports, 'typo' for typo fixes, 'report' for behavior issues, 'request' for feature requests, and 'suggest' for suggestions. Each ticket includes category, subject, description, status, and staff responses when resolved.
tie:
Bind a helpless or restrained character by tying their hands or feet. This command is used to immobilize someone who is unconscious, defeated, or otherwise unable to resist. You can tie just their hands, just their feet, or leave it unspecified (defaults to hands). When you tie someone, other characters in the room see the action, and the bound character is notified. Tying someone requires them to be in a helpless state - you can't tie someone who is actively resisting.

Use 'tie <character>', 'tie <character> hands', or 'tie <character> feet' to bind them. For example: 'tie Bob', 'bind Jane hands', or 'restrain Bob feet'. The command checks if you're trying to change the timeline (some restraint mechanics are disabled in past timelines). Once tied, the character remains bound until untied or they escape.
time:
