The time command displays the current in-game date and time, along with celestial information like moon phase, sun position, cloud cover, and time of day period (morning, afternoon, evening, night, dawn, dusk). The game world has its own calendar and time system that advances independently of real-world time, creating immersive day-night cycles and seasonal changes.

The display shows you a formatted timestamp ('It is 3:45 PM on Wednesday, March 15, 2024 (Afternoon)') followed by relevant astronomical details. At night you'll see the current moon phase with emoji (🌕 full moon, 🌑 new moon, etc.) and whether stars are visible through clouds. During the day you see sun and cloud cover percentage. The time system affects visibility, NPC behavior, shop hours, event scheduling, and various game mechanics that respond to the time of day. This is essential for planning activities, coordinating with others across time zones, and understanding environmental conditions.
timeline:
Manage and enter different timelines for historical roleplay and alternate reality scenarios. This system lets you create snapshots of the current moment that you (and others present) can return to later, or enter historical timelines set in specific years. When in a past timeline, certain mechanics are restricted: death is disabled, prisoner mechanics don't work, you can't gain XP, and rooms are read-only. You can play in a timeline while your main character remains active in the present by opening it in a new browser tab.

Use 'timeline' to open the main menu with options to view, create, enter, or leave timelines. Use 'timeline <name>' to quick-enter a specific snapshot by name. For example: 'timeline "Before the battle"' or 'timeline 2024'. The menu lets you create snapshots (capturing this moment), enter your snapshots or those others have shared with you, enter historical timelines by year and zone, view timeline restrictions, and delete old snapshots.
trash:
The trash command permanently destroys an item from your inventory, deleting it from the game entirely with no way to recover it. This is different from 'drop' which leaves the item on the ground - trash means the item is gone forever. Use this for broken, worthless, or unwanted items you no longer need and want to remove from existence.

This is useful for cleaning up your inventory of damaged equipment, trash items, or things you know you'll never use again. The command is irreversible and intentional - once you trash something, there's no getting it back, so use it carefully. Others in the room see you discard the item, but it doesn't leave anything behind on the ground. You cannot trash money items this way; you'll need to use 'drop' instead if you want to abandon currency.
unpuppet:
Release control of an NPC that you're currently puppeting. This staff command stops your direct control of an NPC, returning them to autonomous AI behavior or idle state. You can release a specific NPC by name or release all NPCs you're puppeting at once. When you unpuppet an NPC, they remain in the game world but are no longer under your direct control - their actions will be driven by their AI personality instead.

Use 'unpuppet' or 'unpuppet all' to release all puppeted NPCs, or 'unpuppet <npc name>' to release a specific one. For example: 'unpuppet Bob' or 'release merchant'. If you're puppeting multiple NPCs and use a partial name, the command will find the best match among your current puppets. You must be actively puppeting at least one NPC for this command to work.
untie:
Remove restraints from a bound character by untying them. This command lets you free someone who has been tied up, gagged, or blindfolded. You can remove specific restraints (hands, feet, gag, blindfold) or remove all restraints at once by using 'all' or leaving it unspecified. When you untie someone, other characters in the room see the action, and the freed character is notified. The command works on any restrained character, regardless of who tied them originally.

Use 'untie <character>' to remove all restraints, or 'untie <character> <type>' to remove specific ones. For example: 'untie Bob' frees him completely, 'untie Jane hands' just unties her hands, 'unbind Bob feet' unties his feet, or 'free Jane gag' removes her gag. You can specify hands, feet, gag, blindfold, or all as the restraint type.
unzip:
Unzip or unbutton clothing items you're wearing. This command opens zipped or buttoned garments, changing their fastened state without removing them. You can unzip single items or multiple items at once by listing them separated by commas or 'and'. The command only works on items you're currently wearing that are in a zipped/buttoned state - items that are already unzipped will be skipped. Other characters in the room see what you're doing.

Use 'unzip <item>' for single items or 'unzip <item1>, <item2>' for multiple. For example: 'unzip jacket', 'unbutton shirt', 'unzip jacket and hoodie', or 'undo dress'. Aliases include 'unbutton', 'undo', 'tear open', and 'rip open'. When processing multiple items, the command will report successes and failures, telling you which items were unzipped and which couldn't be (either not worn or already unzipped).
up:
Move up to a room above your current location. This vertical movement command moves your character one level upward, such as climbing stairs, ascending a ladder, or going to a higher floor. The command only works if there's a valid exit upward from your current location - if the way is blocked by walls, locked doors, or if no upward exit exists, you won't be able to move. Vertical movement is common in multi-story buildings and structures.

Use 'up' or the shortcut 'u' to move upward. This is functionally equivalent to 'move up' or 'walk up' but faster to type. Use the 'exits' command to see which directions are available from your current room, including vertical ones like up and down.
use:
The use command is your Swiss Army knife for interacting with items in your inventory or playing games in the room. When used on an item, it opens an interactive menu showing all available actions - you can hold it, wear it, consume it (if food/drink/smokeable), drop it, examine it, give it to someone, or show it off. This makes 'use' a convenient hub for accessing many other commands through a user-friendly interface.

The command also handles playing games like dartboards, pool tables, card games, or any other interactive entertainment objects. Just 'use [game name]' to play, and if the game has multiple difficulty levels or play styles (like 'aggressive' or 'careful'), you can specify that too: 'use dartboard aggressive'. Many games track your high scores and let you compete with others or challenge yourself. If a game has multiple play modes and you don't specify one, you'll get a menu to choose. You can even reset your score on a game with 'use [game] reset' if you want to start fresh.
viewmemory:
View the complete details of a world memory including all involved characters, locations, importance rating, publicity level, and the full raw log of events. This staff command provides deep access to memory records for investigation, research, or story continuity verification. You can see who participated (with their roles and message counts), which locations were involved, when the memory occurred, and read the actual transcript of what happened.

Use 'viewmemory <memory_id>', 'memview <id>', or 'showmemory <id>' with a numeric memory ID. For example: 'viewmemory 142' or 'memview 98'. The command shows summary, importance, publicity, timeline, character involvement, locations, and the complete raw log (if available and not expired). This is particularly useful after finding relevant memories with 'searchmemory'.
wake:
Wake an unconscious character by shaking them until they regain consciousness. This command is used to revive someone who has been knocked out in combat, is sleeping, or is otherwise unconscious. You can only wake characters who are in the same room as you, and the character must be truly unconscious (not just resting or pretending). When you wake someone, other characters in the room see your action, and the unconscious character receives a message as they come to.

Use 'wake <character>', 'rouse <name>', or 'awaken <name>' to wake someone. For example: 'wake Bob' or 'rouse Jane'. You cannot wake yourself - if you're unconscious, you'll need someone else to help you or wait for natural recovery. The command requires the target to be in a helpless unconscious state.
walk:
The walk command is your primary method of moving through the game world. It's remarkably flexible, allowing you to travel by direction (north, south), destination (walk to tavern), toward other characters (walk to Bob), or to specific furniture or places in the room. The command uses intelligent pathfinding to navigate you through multiple rooms if needed, displaying your progress as you travel.

One of walk's most charming features is its support for movement style through verb aliases - you can 'stroll' casually, 'sprint' urgently, 'sneak' stealthily, or 'stagger' if injured, and these adverbs will be reflected in how others see your movement. When you specify a complex target like a building or distant location, the system calculates an optimal path and moves you step by step, showing each room you pass through with timing that reflects the distance traveled. If your target is ambiguous, you'll get a menu to clarify which destination you mean. Note that you cannot leave a room while engaged in combat - you'll need to resolve or flee from the fight first.
wardrobe:
Comprehensive wardrobe management system for storing, retrieving, and transferring items between your various storage locations. This command provides access to a magical wardrobe system that lets you keep items at different properties without carrying everything with you. You can store items from your inventory, retrieve them when needed, transfer items between locations (with a 12-hour delay), and check transfer status. The wardrobe is accessible from your home or any storage facility.

Use 'wardrobe' to open the main menu, 'store <item>' to store an item, 'retrieve <item>' to get an item, or 'transfer from <location>' to move items between properties. Quick shortcuts: 'stash sword' to store, 'fetch jacket' to retrieve. For example: 'wardrobe store all', 'retrieve leather armor', or 'transfer from apartment'. The system tracks items at each location and shows transfer progress for items in transit.
wear:
The wear command lets you put on clothing, jewelry, and piercings from your inventory. Simply use 'wear [item]' and it will be added to your visible appearance - others will see it when they look at you. The command supports wearing multiple items at once by separating them with commas or 'and', like 'wear hat, scarf, gloves' to dress up quickly.

Piercings require special handling since you need to specify where to wear them. Use 'wear [piercing] on [position]' like 'wear gold ring on left ear'. If you have multiple piercing holes available, the command will show you a menu to choose which one. The system tracks what body positions you have pierced and ensures jewelry goes in the right places. When you don't specify what to wear, you'll get a helpful menu showing all your wearable items organized by type (clothing, jewelry, piercings) to make selection easy.
weather:
The weather command shows you the current atmospheric conditions and climate information for your location. When you check the weather, you'll see an immersive prose description of what the sky and air feel like, followed by detailed meteorological data including temperature (in both Celsius and Fahrenheit), wind speed, humidity, and cloud cover percentage. The system uses real-time weather simulation that varies by location and time of day.

You can only check weather when you're in a location where the sky is visible - if you're indoors or underground, the command will tell you that you can't see the sky. Weather affects gameplay in various ways: severe storms might reduce visibility, extreme temperatures could impact comfort, and certain weather conditions might influence travel or outdoor activities. The command has several aliases like 'forecast' and 'conditions' that work the same way.
west:
Move west to an adjacent room. This is a quick directional movement command that instantly moves your character one room in the western direction. The command only works if there's a valid exit to the west from your current location - if the way is blocked by walls, locked doors, or if no exit exists in that direction, you won't be able to move. Use the 'exits' command to see which directions are available from your current room.

Use 'west' or the shortcut 'w' to move west. This is functionally equivalent to 'move west' or 'walk west' but faster to type. If you need to check where west leads before moving, use the 'look' command to see exit descriptions and what rooms are connected in each direction.
whisper:
The whisper command lets you speak privately to another character in the same room, with only you and your target hearing the full message. Others nearby see that you're whispering but can't hear what you say - they only see an obscured message like 'Alice whispers something to Bob.' This creates intimate, semi-private communication perfect for secret exchanges, conspiracies, or quiet conversations.

You can add adverbs for flavor ('whisper quietly', 'whisper urgently') and the command supports context tracking so repeated whispers to the same person feel natural. If you don't specify a target, you'll get a menu of everyone present in the room. The whispered message is saved in roleplay logs for later review, and the command includes anti-spam validation to prevent abuse. NPCs in the room may react to the fact that you're whispering (the social act) even though they can't hear the content. This makes whisper useful for both mechanical privacy and roleplaying furtive communication.
who:
The who command shows you which other players are currently online and where they are in the game world. By default, 'who' shows everyone online in your current zone, grouped by room and showing their stance and position (sitting at bar, standing by window, etc.). You can expand your view with 'who all' to see everyone across the entire game world, organized by zone, events, and timelines, or narrow it down with 'who here' to see only people in your current room.

The command respects privacy settings and location visibility - characters in private mode or secluded rooms won't appear unless you have permission to see them, and you'll always see your own alternate characters regardless of their settings. When you use 'who all', you'll also see information about upcoming public events if any are scheduled, making this a great way to stay connected with the game's activity and plan where to find roleplay partners. Timeline and event participants are clearly labeled, helping you understand the different narrative spaces people are exploring.
windows:
Toggle the curtains or blinds on your windows, opening or closing them. This command lets you control window coverings in rooms you own, changing the room's ambiance and potentially affecting visibility from outside. When you use this command, it automatically switches the curtain state - if they're open, it closes them; if they're closed, it opens them. Other characters in the room see your action when you adjust the window coverings.

Use 'windows' or 'curtains' to toggle the window coverings in your current room. For example: 'windows' to open or close them. You must own the room to use this command - you can't adjust window coverings in someone else's property. The command works as a simple toggle without needing to specify 'open' or 'close'.
withdraw:
The withdraw command transfers money from your bank account into your wallet as physical cash that you carry. You must be standing at a bank location or ATM to use this command - you can't withdraw remotely. Type 'withdraw' followed by an amount like 'withdraw 100', or use 'withdraw all' to take out everything in your account. The game will show you the formatted currency amount and update both your bank balance and wallet balance.

There's a minimum withdrawal amount enforced by the banking system, and you obviously can't withdraw more than your account contains. Other characters in the room will see you withdraw cash, but they won't see the exact amount. Once money is in your wallet, it's physical currency you're carrying - this means it could potentially be stolen or dropped if you're robbed or die, unlike money safely stored in your bank account. Use 'balance' to check both your wallet and bank balances, and 'deposit' to put money back into the bank for safekeeping.
zipup:
Zip up or button clothing items you're wearing. This command fastens zipped or buttoned garments, changing them from an open state to a closed state without removing them. You can zip up single items or multiple items at once by listing them separated by commas or 'and'. The command only works on items you're currently wearing that are in an unzipped/unbuttoned state - items that are already zipped up will be skipped. Other characters in the room see what you're doing.

Use 'zipup <item>' for single items or 'zipup <item1>, <item2>' for multiple. For example: 'zipup jacket', 'button shirt', 'zip hoodie', 'zipup jacket and coat', or 'button shirt, vest'. Aliases include 'zip', 'button', 'do up', and 'button up'. When processing multiple items, the command will report successes and failures, telling you which items were zipped up and which couldn't be (either not worn or already zipped).
